front: Probability space (1.1 Probability Spaces)
back: A probability model is a triple (Omega, F, P): outcomes, a sigma-field of events, and a countably additive probability measure with total mass 1. Exam use: Use this as the opening language for proofs involving events, limsup/liminf events, and Borel-Cantelli later in Chapter 2. Pitfall: Do not treat arbitrary subsets as events unless F is the full power set; measurability is part of the model.

front: Sigma-field closure rules (1.1 Probability Spaces)
back: A sigma-field is closed under complements and countable unions, hence also under countable intersections and finite set operations. Exam use: When proving a random object is measurable, show that a convenient generating class of inverse images lies in F. Pitfall: A countable union is allowed; an arbitrary uncountable union need not be in the sigma-field.

front: Continuity of measure (1.1 Probability Spaces)
back: For increasing events A_n, P(union A_n) is the limit of P(A_n). For decreasing events A_n with finite first measure, P(intersection A_n) is the limit of P(A_n). Exam use: Essential for limits of events, almost sure convergence criteria, and first Borel-Cantelli arguments. Pitfall: For decreasing continuity, the finite-measure condition matters for general measures; it is automatic under probability measures.

front: Discrete probability spaces (1.1 Probability Spaces)
back: On a countable sample space, assign nonnegative masses p(omega) summing to 1, and define P(A) as the sum over omega in A. Exam use: Useful for checking intuition before proving the analogous statement for general measures. Pitfall: If the sample space is uncountable, point masses need not determine the whole law.

front: Stieltjes measures from distribution-like functions (1.1 Probability Spaces)
back: A nondecreasing right-continuous function with suitable limits defines a unique measure on the Borel line via interval increments. Exam use: This is the bridge from CDFs to probability measures on R. Pitfall: Right-continuity is not cosmetic; it pins down the measure on half-infinite intervals and atoms.

front: Extension from semialgebras (1.1 Probability Spaces)
back: A measure-like set function on a semialgebra can be extended when it has the right countable additivity/continuity behavior on the generating class. Exam use: Explains why specifying probabilities on intervals or rectangles is enough in many probability models. Pitfall: Do not assume every finitely additive premeasure extends; the countable condition is the serious part.

front: Multidimensional distribution functions (1.1 Probability Spaces)
back: A function on R^d satisfying monotonicity over rectangles, right-continuity, and correct limits defines a probability measure on Borel R^d. Exam use: This underlies joint distributions and later independence statements. Pitfall: Coordinatewise monotonicity alone is not enough; rectangle probabilities must be nonnegative.

front: Distribution function properties (1.2 Distributions)
back: A CDF is nondecreasing, right-continuous, tends to 0 at -infinity, and tends to 1 at +infinity. Exam use: Use these properties to test whether a proposed function can be a CDF. Pitfall: Left-continuity is generally false; jumps represent atoms.

front: Every valid CDF determines a law (1.2 Distributions)
back: Any function with the standard CDF properties is the distribution function of a unique probability measure on R. Exam use: Lets you prove convergence claims by identifying limiting CDFs, provided the limit is a valid CDF at continuity points. Pitfall: A pointwise limit of CDFs may fail to be a CDF if total mass escapes to infinity.

front: Atoms are jumps of the CDF (1.2 Distributions)
back: For a real random variable, P(X = x) equals the jump F(x) - F(x-) of its distribution function. Exam use: Useful for mixed discrete-continuous examples and for proving a CDF has at most countably many discontinuities. Pitfall: A continuous CDF does not imply a density exists; the Cantor distribution is the standard warning.

front: Standard normal tail estimate (1.2 Distributions)
back: The standard normal upper tail has exponential decay comparable to phi(x)/x for positive x. Exam use: Appears in Brownian motion, LIL-style bounds, and CLT error estimates. Pitfall: Keep track of whether a bound is one-sided or two-sided; prelim solutions often lose constants harmlessly but not exponents.

front: Probability integral transform (1.2 Distributions)
back: If X has a continuous CDF F, then F(X) is uniform on (0,1) under suitable strictness/continuity conditions. Exam use: Useful for constructing random variables from uniform variables and for distribution transformations. Pitfall: Continuity alone needs care when F has flat intervals; use generalized inverses for the clean general statement.

front: Random variables as measurable maps (1.3 Random Variables)
back: A random variable is a measurable map from the sample space to a measurable state space. Exam use: This is the language needed for sigma(X), conditional expectation, and stochastic processes. Pitfall: Do not confuse a random variable with its distribution; different variables may have the same law.

front: Checking measurability on generators (1.3 Random Variables)
back: To prove X is measurable into a generated sigma-field, it is enough to check inverse images of a generating class. Exam use: Use half-lines for real variables and rectangles for vector-valued variables. Pitfall: The generator must actually generate the target sigma-field; checking too small a class proves too little.

front: Composition of measurable maps (1.3 Random Variables)
back: If X is measurable and f is measurable, then f(X) is measurable. Exam use: Use this to justify transformations such as X^2, exp(X), indicators, vectors, sums, limsup, and liminf. Pitfall: Continuity is sufficient for Borel measurability, but not necessary.

front: Arithmetic of random variables (1.3 Random Variables)
back: Finite sums, products, maxima, minima, and continuous functions of random variables are random variables. Exam use: Often needed before taking expectations of constructed quantities. Pitfall: Do not use arithmetic closure for extended real expressions without checking undefined forms like infinity minus infinity.

front: Limits preserve measurability (1.3 Random Variables)
back: Supremum, infimum, limsup, liminf, and pointwise limits of measurable real functions are measurable. Exam use: Crucial for defining almost sure convergence and stopping-time events. Pitfall: The countability of the sequence matters; arbitrary suprema over uncountable families can fail measurability.

front: Functions measurable with respect to sigma(X) (1.3 Random Variables)
back: A random variable Y is sigma(X)-measurable exactly when it can be represented as a measurable function of X. Exam use: This is a foundation for conditional expectation as a function of observed information. Pitfall: The representing function is only determined up to the distribution of X, not necessarily pointwise everywhere.

front: Simple functions (1.4 Integration)
back: A simple function takes finitely many values and is the starting point for defining the Lebesgue integral. Exam use: When stuck on an integral proof, first prove it for indicators, then simple functions, then limits. Pitfall: Representations of a simple function are not unique; use canonical level sets to avoid ambiguity.

front: Integral of a nonnegative function (1.4 Integration)
back: The integral of a nonnegative measurable function is the supremum of integrals of simple functions bounded above by it. Exam use: This definition makes monotone convergence natural rather than surprising. Pitfall: The value may be infinite; do not subtract two infinite nonnegative integrals.

front: Integrable signed functions (1.4 Integration)
back: A signed measurable function is integrable when the integrals of its positive and negative parts are finite, equivalently the integral of its absolute value is finite. Exam use: Before using linearity or dominated convergence, check integrability conditions. Pitfall: Finite positive and negative parts are needed; infinity minus infinity is not an acceptable value.

front: Linearity and order properties of the integral (1.4 Integration)
back: For integrable functions, the integral is linear, monotone, and respects almost-everywhere equality. Exam use: Use to split expectations, compare nonnegative variables, and justify replacing variables by a.s. equal versions. Pitfall: Linearity can fail as a finite statement if one side involves undefined infinities.

front: Jensen's inequality for integrals (1.5 Properties of the Integral)
back: For a convex function phi and a probability measure, phi(integral f) is at most integral phi(f), assuming the quantities are defined. Exam use: Prelim workhorse for Lp comparisons, moment generating bounds, and conditional Jensen later. Pitfall: Convexity direction matters; strict equality often encodes almost sure constancy under strict convexity.

front: Holder's inequality (1.5 Properties of the Integral)
back: If p and q are conjugate exponents, the integral of |fg| is bounded by the Lp norm of f times the Lq norm of g. Exam use: Used for moment interpolation, uniform integrability checks, and bounding expectations of products. Pitfall: Remember the endpoint cases need separate interpretation.

front: Minkowski's inequality (1.5 Properties of the Integral)
back: The Lp norm satisfies the triangle inequality for p at least 1. Exam use: Use to prove Lp spaces are normed and to control sums of random variables in Lp. Pitfall: For p below 1 this is not a norm inequality.

front: Bounded convergence theorem (1.5 Properties of the Integral)
back: On a finite-measure space, uniformly bounded functions converging pointwise have integrals converging to the integral of the limit. Exam use: Useful for probability spaces when random variables are uniformly bounded. Pitfall: Finite measure is essential; bounded pointwise convergence on an infinite measure space need not preserve integrals.

front: Fatou's lemma (1.5 Properties of the Integral)
back: For nonnegative functions, the integral of the liminf is at most the liminf of the integrals. Exam use: Use for lower semicontinuity of expectations and to prove integrability of a limit from bounded expectations. Pitfall: Fatou gives an inequality, not usually equality.

front: Monotone convergence theorem (1.5 Properties of the Integral)
back: If nonnegative measurable functions increase pointwise to f, then their integrals increase to the integral of f. Exam use: Use for exchanging sums and expectations of nonnegative random variables. Pitfall: The monotone direction and nonnegativity are not optional unless you shift or dominate carefully.

front: Dominated convergence theorem (1.5 Properties of the Integral)
back: If f_n converges pointwise a.e. to f and |f_n| is bounded by an integrable g, then integrals of f_n converge to the integral of f. Exam use: The main theorem for passing limits through expectations under a uniform integrable bound. Pitfall: Pointwise boundedness is not enough; the dominating function must be integrable and independent of n.

front: Expected value as Lebesgue integral (1.6 Expected Value)
back: Expectation is the integral of a random variable with respect to the probability measure. Exam use: This unifies discrete sums, density integrals, and abstract random variables. Pitfall: An expectation can be undefined if positive and negative parts are both infinite.

front: Linearity and monotonicity of expectation (1.6 Expected Value)
back: Expectation is linear for integrable random variables and monotone for ordered random variables. Exam use: Use constantly in variance, covariance, martingale, and conditioning calculations. Pitfall: Linearity does not require independence; product factorization does.

front: Markov and Chebyshev inequalities (1.6 Expected Value)
back: Markov bounds the probability of a nonnegative variable exceeding a threshold by its expectation over the threshold; Chebyshev applies this to powers or centered moments. Exam use: Core tool for convergence in probability, LLN proofs, and variance bounds. Pitfall: Check nonnegativity and choose the right moment; a weak moment gives a weak tail bound.

front: Limit theorems for expectation (1.6 Expected Value)
back: Fatou, monotone convergence, dominated convergence, and bounded convergence all have expectation versions. Exam use: A prelim proof often hinges on naming exactly the right convergence theorem and verifying its hypotheses. Pitfall: Do not cite dominated convergence when you only have bounded expectations; that is a uniform integrability issue, not automatic domination.

front: Change of variables formula (1.6 Expected Value)
back: If X has law mu and f is measurable, then E[f(X)] equals the integral of f with respect to mu, when the positive or integrable conditions hold. Exam use: Lets you compute expectations using the distribution rather than the underlying sample space. Pitfall: Do not assume a density exists; the law may be discrete, continuous, singular, or mixed.

front: Tail integral and nonnegative expectations (1.6 Expected Value)
back: For nonnegative random variables, expectations can often be computed or bounded through tail probabilities. Exam use: Important for moment bounds, heavy-tail examples, and Borel-Cantelli estimates. Pitfall: Mind strict versus non-strict inequalities only at atoms; the integral is unaffected in many continuous-parameter formulas.

front: Common expectation computations (1.6 Expected Value)
back: Chapter 1 reviews expectations for exponential, normal, Bernoulli, Poisson, and geometric distributions. Exam use: These examples are quick diagnostic checks for later characteristic-function and limit problems. Pitfall: Parameterization varies, especially for geometric distributions; state your convention.

front: Inclusion-exclusion and Bonferroni (1.6 Expected Value)
back: The probability of a finite union can be expanded by alternating sums of intersection probabilities, with Bonferroni inequalities for truncations. Exam use: Useful for event-counting, occupancy-style estimates, and bounding union probabilities more sharply than the union bound. Pitfall: The exact formula is finite; infinite versions require additional limiting arguments.

front: Product measures (1.7 Product Measures, Fubini's Theorem)
back: Given measure spaces, the product measure is the unique measure on the product sigma-field that agrees with products of measures on measurable rectangles. Exam use: The measure-theoretic basis for joint distributions and independence. Pitfall: A set in the product sigma-field can be much more complicated than a rectangle.

front: Measurable sections (1.7 Product Measures, Fubini's Theorem)
back: For a measurable set in a product space, its x-section is measurable in the second space, and section measures are measurable functions of x. Exam use: This is the technical engine behind Fubini and Tonelli. Pitfall: Do not assume section results for nonmeasurable product subsets.

front: Fubini and Tonelli theorem (1.7 Product Measures, Fubini's Theorem)
back: For nonnegative functions, iterated integrals and the product integral agree. For signed functions, the same holds when the absolute integral is finite. Exam use: Use to exchange sums/integrals/expectations and compute expectations by conditioning-like iterated integration. Pitfall: Tonelli handles nonnegative functions even with infinite value; Fubini for signed functions needs integrability.

front: Product measures as preview of independence (1.7 Product Measures, Fubini's Theorem)
back: When a joint law factors as a product measure, the coordinates behave independently. Exam use: This prepares for Chapter 2 independence and for computing expectations of products. Pitfall: Independence is a statement about the joint law, not merely about marginal distributions.

front: Countable/co-countable probability measure (1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras)
back: To verify the countable/co-countable construction, split disjoint families into the cases where all sets are countable or exactly one set is co-countable. Exam use: Exercise 1.1.1 and any probability-space verification on unusual sigma-fields. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Borel sigma-field from half-open rectangles (1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras)
back: Show half-open rectangles are Borel, then express rational open rectangles as countable unions of half-open rectangles; use the countable rational base. Exam use: Exercises 1.1.2 and 1.1.3; product-measure generation arguments. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Increasing union of sigma-fields can fail countable closure (1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras)
back: Use finite initial-coordinate sigma-fields on N; their union contains every finite set but misses an infinite non-cofinite set such as the evens. Exam use: Exercise 1.1.4; distinguishing algebras from sigma-fields. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Block construction for asymptotic density counterexamples (1.1 Exercises: Probability spaces, sigma-fields, generators, and set algebras)
back: Use rapidly growing blocks so the latest block dominates all previous blocks; alternate behavior by blocks to destroy the density of intersections. Exam use: Exercise 1.1.5 and density/limit counterexamples. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Piecewise random variable measurability (1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations)
back: For Z = X on A and Y on A^c, write {Z <= x} as (A cap {X <= x}) union (A^c cap {Y <= x}). Exam use: Exercise 1.2.1; patching random variables on measurable events. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Countability of CDF jumps (1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations)
back: Group jumps by size bigger than 1/m inside bounded intervals; each group is finite because total CDF increase is at most one. Exam use: Exercise 1.2.3; monotone-function discontinuity arguments. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Monotone density change of variables (1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations)
back: For Y=g(X) with g increasing, compute F_Y(y)=P(X <= g^{-1}(y)) and differentiate to get f_X(g^{-1}(y))/g'(g^{-1}(y)). Exam use: Exercises 1.2.5 and 1.2.6; lognormal and affine density transforms. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Density of a square transform (1.2 Exercises: Distributions, normal tails, transformations, and density calculations)
back: For Y=X^2, use F_Y(y)=P(-sqrt(y)<=X<=sqrt(y)) and differentiate to get [f(sqrt(y))+f(-sqrt(y))]/(2sqrt(y)). Exam use: Exercise 1.2.7; chi-square with one degree of freedom. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Rational cut proof for sum measurability (1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization)
back: Write {X+Y<x} as the countable union over q in Q of {X<q} cap {Y<x-q}. Exam use: Exercise 1.3.2; direct measurability of sums. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Lower semicontinuity via closed sublevel sets (1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization)
back: A function is lower semicontinuous iff all sets {f <= a} are closed; sequential closure proves one direction and contradiction via subsequences proves the other. Exam use: Exercise 1.3.5; proving semicontinuous functions are measurable. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Discontinuity set from upper and lower local envelopes (1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization)
back: Define local sup and inf over balls, take limits as radius decreases, and identify discontinuities as the set where the limiting envelopes differ. Exam use: Exercise 1.3.6; measurability of discontinuity sets. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Constructive representation of sigma(X)-measurable functions (1.3 Exercises: Measurability, generated sigma-fields, semicontinuity, and sigma(X) factorization)
back: Quantize Y into dyadic bins, represent each bin as {X in B}, build Borel simple functions f_n, then take a measurable pointwise limit f with Y=f(X). Exam use: Exercises 1.3.8 and 1.3.9; conditional-expectation foundations. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Zero integral of nonnegative function implies zero a.e. (1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation)
back: Use sets {f > 1/n}; each has zero measure because integral dominates (1/n) times its measure, and their union is {f>0}. Exam use: Exercise 1.4.1; null-set proofs. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Dyadic lower simple approximations (1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation)
back: Approximate f>=0 by 2^{-n} floor(2^n f); the approximants increase to f and their integrals give monotone sums over dyadic level sets. Exam use: Exercise 1.4.2 and simple-function approximation. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: L1 approximation by continuous functions (1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation)
back: Approximate an integrable function by simple functions, approximate measurable sets by finite interval unions, then round step-function corners. Exam use: Exercise 1.4.3; density of nice functions in L1 on R. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Riemann-Lebesgue by step-function reduction (1.4 Exercises: Lebesgue integration construction and approximation)
back: Prove oscillatory integral decay for interval indicators explicitly, then approximate any L1 function by a step function. Exam use: Exercise 1.4.4; oscillatory integral estimates. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Essential supremum bounds integrals (1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral)
back: If |g| <= ||g||_infty a.e., then |fg| <= ||g||_infty |f| and integration gives the L1-Linfty Holder endpoint. Exam use: Exercise 1.5.1; endpoint Holder inequalities. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Lp norms converge to Linfty on probability spaces (1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral)
back: Upper bound by essential supremum; lower bound using sets where |f| exceeds M-epsilon and their positive probability. Exam use: Exercise 1.5.2; norm comparisons. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Simple functions are dense in Lp (1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral)
back: Use pointwise simple approximations bounded by |f|, then dominated convergence on |phi_n-f|^p. Exam use: Exercise 1.5.9; Lp approximation. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Exchange integral and absolutely summable series (1.5 Exercises: Lp inequalities, convergence theorems, and absolute continuity of the integral)
back: If sum integral |f_n| is finite, then H=sum |f_n| is integrable; dominated convergence applies to partial sums. Exam use: Exercise 1.5.10 and 1.7.1 corollary. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Equality case in strict Jensen (1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits)
back: For strictly convex phi, equality in Jensen forces the random variable to be almost surely constant at its mean. Exam use: Exercise 1.6.1; strict convexity equality cases. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Two-point distributions as Chebyshev extremizers (1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits)
back: To prove sharpness of moment/tail inequalities, place small mass at boundary points and the rest at a value chosen to match moments. Exam use: Exercises 1.6.3, 1.6.4, and 1.6.5. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Cauchy-Schwarz lower bound for positive probability (1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits)
back: For Y>=0, Cauchy-Schwarz applied to Y*1_{Y>0} yields P(Y>0) >= (EY)^2/EY^2. Exam use: Exercise 1.6.6; basic Paley-Zygmund-style estimates. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Tail truncation for expectation limits (1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits)
back: Control small or large tails by splitting the event and using continuity of probability plus elementary bounds on the integrand. Exam use: Exercise 1.6.14; limits involving y E(1/X; X>y). Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Indicator expansion for inclusion-exclusion and Bonferroni (1.6 Exercises: Expectation, Jensen/Chebyshev, moment bounds, and expectation limits)
back: Use 1_union = 1 - product_i(1-1_Ai), expand, and take expectations; partial alternating sums give Bonferroni bounds. Exam use: Exercises 1.6.9 and 1.6.10. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Layer-cake formula via product sets (1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration)
back: Represent g(x) as the vertical length of {(x,y):0 <= y < g(x)} and apply Tonelli to integrate sections. Exam use: Exercise 1.7.2; tail integrals for nonnegative functions. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Stieltjes integration by parts with jump correction (1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration)
back: Interpret Stieltjes integrals as product-measure masses of order regions; the diagonal overlap contributes the product of jump masses. Exam use: Exercise 1.7.3. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Sliding interval Fubini identity (1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration)
back: Write F(x+c)-F(x) as mu((x,x+c]), swap integrals, and observe each fixed t is counted for exactly c units of x. Exam use: Exercise 1.7.4. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Sine integral via Fubini (1.7 Exercises: Product measures, Fubini/Tonelli, layer-cake formulas, and Stieltjes integration)
back: Represent sin(x)/x as an integral of e^{-xy} sin(x), integrate in x first, and bound the remaining exponentially damped tails. Exam use: Exercise 1.7.5. Pitfall: Check the hypotheses before reusing the pattern; many Chapter 1 arguments depend on measurability, integrability, monotonicity, or nonnegativity.

front: Independence of events (2.1 Independence)
back: Events A and B are independent when P(A intersect B) = P(A)P(B); finite families require the product formula for every subfamily. Exam use: Use it as the base language for indicator arguments, Borel-Cantelli, and pairwise-versus-mutual independence examples. Pitfall: Pairwise independence does not imply mutual independence; always check all finite subcollections when the statement says independent.

front: Random variables and sigma-field independence (2.1 Independence)
back: Random variables are independent exactly when their generated sigma-fields are independent; measurable variables inside independent sigma-fields are independent. Exam use: Lets you move cleanly between event, random-variable, and information-field formulations. Pitfall: Do not confuse uncorrelated with independent; zero covariance is far weaker.

front: Indicators encode event independence (2.1 Independence)
back: Events A and B are independent if and only if their indicators 1_A and 1_B are independent random variables. Exam use: Useful when converting event problems into expectation or covariance calculations. Pitfall: Independence of indicators is stronger than merely E[1_A 1_B] being computable; it is equivalent here because the variables are binary.

front: Pi-lambda independence extension (2.1.1 Sufficient Conditions for Independence)
back: If independent collections are pi-systems, then the sigma-fields they generate are independent. Exam use: High-value proof method for showing independence from checking rectangles, half-lines, or finite-dimensional cylinder sets. Pitfall: The checked classes must be pi-systems and must generate the desired sigma-fields.

front: Independence of blocks (2.1.1 Sufficient Conditions for Independence)
back: Disjoint blocks built from independent families are independent after taking generated sigma-fields. Exam use: Use this to show X_1 is independent of functions of X_2,...,X_n, or that future increments are independent of past events. Pitfall: The blocks must be formed from disjoint subfamilies of an independent family.

front: Functions of independent blocks (2.1.1 Sufficient Conditions for Independence)
back: Measurable functions of disjoint independent blocks remain independent. Exam use: Essential for random walks: past maxima and future increments can be separated. Pitfall: The functions may be complicated, but they cannot reuse variables from another block.

front: Independent variables have product joint law (2.1.2 Independence, Distribution, and Expectation)
back: If X_1,...,X_n are independent with laws mu_i, then the joint law of the vector is the product measure mu_1 x ... x mu_n. Exam use: Turns independence questions into product-measure integration questions. Pitfall: Matching marginal laws alone never proves independence; the joint law must factor.

front: Expectation factorization under independence (2.1.2 Independence, Distribution, and Expectation)
back: If X and Y are independent, then E[h(X,Y)] is the product-measure integral; in particular E[f(X)g(Y)] = E[f(X)]E[g(Y)] under nonnegative or integrable hypotheses. Exam use: Core tool for variance of sums, covariance checks, moment computations, and LLN proofs. Pitfall: The factorization needs independence and integrability/nonnegativity; linearity of expectation alone is not enough for products.

front: Convolution of distribution functions (2.1.3 Sums of Independent Random Variables)
back: For independent X and Y with CDFs F and G, P(X+Y <= z) = integral F(z-y) dG(y). Exam use: Use for distribution of sums when densities are absent or mixed. Pitfall: The notation dG means integration with respect to the probability measure induced by G, not ordinary differentiation unless a density exists.

front: Convolution density formula (2.1.3 Sums of Independent Random Variables)
back: If X has density f and Y is independent with law G, then X+Y has density h(x)= integral f(x-y)dG(y); if Y has density g, h=f*g. Exam use: Standard way to compute sums of exponentials, normals, and mixed variables. Pitfall: Keep the order x-y straight and check that the variables are independent.

front: Standard convolution examples (2.1.3 Sums of Independent Random Variables)
back: Independent exponentials with the same rate give gamma/Erlang sums; independent normals add means and variances. Exam use: These are quick recognition patterns for prelim computations and later CLT comparisons. Pitfall: Normal parameters vary by text; in Durrett normal(mu,a) uses variance parameter a.

front: Constructing independent random variables (2.1.4 Constructing Independent Random Variables)
back: Finite independent variables with prescribed laws live on a product space with coordinate maps; infinite sequences use infinite product measures and cylinder sets. Exam use: Important when a proof begins with 'let X_1,X_2,... be independent with laws ...'; this construction justifies existence. Pitfall: Infinite product constructions are subtler than finite products; do not treat all subsets of R^N as measurable.

front: L2 weak law of large numbers (2.2.1 L2 Weak Laws)
back: For uncorrelated variables with bounded average variance, (S_n - E S_n)/n converges to 0 in L2 and hence in probability. Exam use: Fastest LLN proof when second moments are available. Pitfall: Independence is sufficient but not necessary; uncorrelatedness plus variance control is enough.

front: IID finite variance weak law (2.2.1 L2 Weak Laws)
back: If X_i are iid with finite variance and mean mu, then S_n/n converges to mu in probability. Exam use: The canonical first LLN proof; use it whenever the problem gives second moments. Pitfall: This proof does not cover infinite-variance variables, even when the mean exists.

front: Triangular array weak law (2.2.2 Triangular Arrays)
back: For row-wise independent triangular arrays, convergence of sums follows when row variances are small and large jumps are controlled. Exam use: This is the flexible form behind many LLN proofs with changing distributions. Pitfall: Rows are independent within each n; do not assume one infinite sequence unless the problem states it.

front: Truncation method for weak laws (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: Replace X_i by X_i 1{|X_i| <= cutoff}, prove a law for the bounded part, and show discarded large jumps are negligible. Exam use: Main route from finite variance LLN to finite mean LLN. Pitfall: Choose the cutoff at the scale of the sum, often n or epsilon n; a fixed cutoff usually proves the wrong statement.

front: IID finite mean weak law (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: If X_i are iid with E|X_1| < infinity and mean mu, then S_n/n converges to mu in probability. Exam use: This is the weak law most often needed when only integrability is assumed. Pitfall: Finite mean is enough for WLLN but the proof requires controlling both tails and centering errors.

front: Maximum term negligibility (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: For iid integrable variables, max_{i<=n}|X_i|/n goes to 0 in probability. Exam use: Often proves that no single observation can dominate the average. Pitfall: The statement can fail for heavy tails without finite first moment.

front: Weak law example patterns (2.2 Weak Laws of Large Numbers)
back: Chapter 2 uses weak laws to control averages, random counts, and approximations where variance estimates or truncation make the error vanish. Exam use: Use this as a mental index for prelim questions asking for convergence in probability. Pitfall: First identify the normalization; the wrong scale can make a true statement look false.

front: Weighted Cesaro variance weak law (2.2.1 L2 Weak Laws)
back: If uncorrelated variables satisfy var(X_i)/i -> 0, then n^{-2} sum_{i<=n} var(X_i) -> 0, so the centered average converges to 0 in L2. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.1 pattern: use when variances grow sublinearly rather than being uniformly bounded. Pitfall: Do not require identical distributions; the deterministic centering is E S_n/n, not necessarily a fixed mean.

front: Dependent covariance weak law (2.2.1 L2 Weak Laws)
back: A zero-mean sequence can satisfy a weak law even when dependent, provided lag covariances are bounded above by r(k) with r(k)->0. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.2 pattern: independence is not the real requirement; small average covariance is. Pitfall: The hypothesis is an upper bound without absolute values, so use it to control E(S_n^2) from above rather than proving absolute summability.

front: Monte Carlo integration via WLLN (2.2.1 L2 Weak Laws)
back: For iid uniform U_i and integrable f on [0,1], the sample average n^{-1} sum f(U_i) converges in probability to integral_0^1 f. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.3 pattern: translate numerical integration into an average of iid random variables. Pitfall: The finite-variance Chebyshev bound at threshold a/sqrt(n) is order Var(f(U))/a^2, not a vanishing bound unless the threshold is fixed.

front: Oscillating heavy tails can have finite effective centering (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: A distribution may have infinite absolute mean but a convergent truncated mean, allowing S_n/n to converge in probability to a finite constant. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.4 pattern: cancellation in signs can produce a finite weak-law center even when E|X| is infinite. Pitfall: Do not call the limiting center EX unless the positive and negative parts are both finite.

front: Diverging truncated-mean weak law (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: If xP(|X|>x)->0 but EX is infinite, the averages can still satisfy S_n/n - mu_n -> 0 with mu_n=E[X 1{|X|<=n}] diverging. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.5 pattern: separate weak-law concentration from existence of a finite mean. Pitfall: A moving centering sequence is not the same as convergence of S_n/n to a finite constant.

front: Integer-valued tail-sum moment formulas (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: For nonnegative integer-valued X, E X=sum_{n>=1}P(X>=n) and E X^2=sum_{n>=1}(2n-1)P(X>=n). Exam use: Exercise 2.2.6 pattern: convert moments into tail sums for discrete heavy-tail checks. Pitfall: Use >= n for integer variables; continuous formulas use integrals with P(X>t) or P(X>=t), which differ only on negligible sets in many settings.

front: General tail integral transform (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: If H(x)=integral_{(-infty,x]} h(y)dy with h>=0, then E H(X)=integral h(y)P(X>=y)dy. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.7 pattern: derive exponential moment and tail identities without differentiating a distribution function. Pitfall: Nonnegativity of h is what makes Tonelli immediate; signed h needs integrability checks.

front: Unfair fair game truncation scale (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: For rare payoffs of size 2^k with probabilities about 1/(2^k k^2), truncation at b_n=2^{m(n)} can force S_n/(n/log_2 n) to converge to a negative constant despite EX=0. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.8 pattern: a formally fair infinite-mean game can have typical averages far below its expectation. Pitfall: The expectation calculation is not enough; the typical scale is determined by truncation and the tail beyond b_n.

front: Positive-variable self-normalized weak law (2.2.3 Truncation)
back: For nonnegative iid variables, if mu(s)/(s P(X>s))->infinity and b_n solves n mu(b_n)=b_n, then S_n/b_n -> 1 in probability. Exam use: Exercise 2.2.9 pattern: choose the normalization from the truncated first moment rather than from a finite mean. Pitfall: The proof depends on b_n tending to infinity and on controlling xP(X>x) relative to mu(x); do not assume finite mean.

front: First Borel-Cantelli lemma (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: If sum P(A_n) < infinity, then P(A_n infinitely often)=0. Exam use: Use to prove almost sure eventual bounds from summable probability estimates. Pitfall: No independence is needed for the first lemma.

front: Second Borel-Cantelli lemma (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For independent events, if sum P(A_n)=infinity, then P(A_n infinitely often)=1. Exam use: Use to prove infinitely many successes, recurrence-style events, and almost sure lower bounds. Pitfall: Independence or a suitable replacement is essential; divergence alone is not enough.

front: Limsup event interpretation (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: The event {A_n infinitely often} is limsup A_n = intersection_m union_{n>=m} A_n. Exam use: Needed before applying Borel-Cantelli and for a.s. convergence arguments. Pitfall: Do not confuse limsup A_n with union_n A_n; limsup requires infinitely many occurrences.

front: Summable tail bounds imply a.s. eventual control (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: If P(|X_n| > a_n) is summable, then |X_n| <= a_n eventually almost surely. Exam use: A common one-line finish after Markov, Chebyshev, or exponential bounds. Pitfall: Summability of the bound matters; merely tending to zero gives convergence in probability, not a.s. eventual control.

front: Subsequence plus monotonicity upgrade (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: Prove almost sure convergence along a sparse subsequence using Borel-Cantelli, then control gaps by monotonicity or maximal bounds. Exam use: Appears in strong law proofs and renewal estimates. Pitfall: The bridge is the hard part; convergence on a subsequence alone usually does not imply full convergence.

front: Independent trials occur infinitely often (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For independent events with non-summable probabilities, the events occur infinitely often with probability one. Exam use: Useful for coin-flip patterns, record events, and rare-event recurrence questions. Pitfall: Verify mutual independence or an acceptable independent-block structure, not just pairwise independence.

front: Subsequence principle for convergence in probability (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: X_n -> X in probability iff every subsequence has a further subsequence converging to X almost surely. Exam use: Exercises 2.3.4-2.3.5 pattern: reduce convergence-in-probability limit theorems to almost sure subsequences. Pitfall: The almost sure subsequence depends on the original subsequence; this does not say the whole sequence converges a.s.

front: Continuous mapping for convergence in probability (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: If X_n -> X in probability and f is continuous, then f(X_n) -> f(X) in probability. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.2 pattern: prove mapping results directly without invoking subsequences. Pitfall: Continuity only gives local uniform continuity after compact localization; global uniform continuity is not required.

front: Fatou and dominated convergence from convergence in probability (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: Fatou and dominated convergence extend from a.s. convergence to convergence in probability by passing to a.s. convergent subsequences. Exam use: Exercises 2.3.4-2.3.5 pattern: combine subsequence extraction with Fatou, DCT, bounded convergence, or uniform integrability. Pitfall: For unbounded dominated-convergence variants, prove uniform integrability; convergence in probability alone is not enough for expectations.

front: Metric and completeness for convergence in probability (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: The metric E[|X-Y|/(1+|X-Y|)] metrizes convergence in probability, and random variables modulo a.s. equality are complete under it. Exam use: Exercises 2.3.6-2.3.7 pattern: turn Cauchy in probability into an a.s. Cauchy subsequence, then pull the whole sequence back by Cauchy control. Pitfall: The metric is on equivalence classes modulo a.s. equality; otherwise d(X,Y)=0 is only equality a.s.

front: Kochen-Stone second-moment lower bound (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: If sum P(A_n)=infinity, then P(A_n i.o.) is bounded below by a limsup ratio of squared expected counts to second moments of event counts. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.10 pattern: prove lower bounds for infinitely-often events under dependence when full independence is unavailable. Pitfall: The denominator uses all pairwise intersections; replacing it by product probabilities is only valid under independence.

front: Bernoulli convergence via Borel-Cantelli (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For independent Bernoulli variables X_n with P(X_n=1)=p_n, X_n -> 0 in probability iff p_n -> 0, and X_n -> 0 a.s. iff sum p_n < infinity. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.11 pattern: reduce binary convergence questions to summability of success probabilities. Pitfall: The a.s. direction needs independence for the divergent case.

front: On countable spaces, convergence in probability implies a.s. convergence (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: If Omega is countable with all subsets measurable, convergence in probability implies pointwise convergence on all atoms of positive probability, hence almost sure convergence. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.12 pattern: use atoms to upgrade convergence modes. Pitfall: This relies on countability; it is false on general probability spaces.

front: Arbitrary random variables can be normalized to zero a.s. (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For any random variables X_n, one can choose deterministic c_n -> infinity so that X_n/c_n -> 0 almost surely. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.13 pattern: build a normalization after seeing the sequence rather than using moment assumptions. Pitfall: The constants are not canonical and may grow very fast.

front: Independent supremum finiteness criterion (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For independent X_n, sup_n X_n is finite a.s. iff sum P(X_n>A)<infinity for some finite A. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.14 pattern: characterize boundedness of an independent sequence by summability of one tail level. Pitfall: Independence is used only for the divergent necessity direction.

front: Exponential maxima live at log n scale (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For iid exponential-tail variables with P(X>x)=e^{-x}, limsup X_n/log n=1 and max_{m<=n}X_m/log n -> 1 almost surely. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.15 pattern: identify extreme-value scales via summable/nonsummable tail probabilities. Pitfall: Pointwise limsup and running maximum convergence require slightly different lower-bound arguments.

front: Threshold maxima infinitely-often criterion (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For iid X_n and increasing thresholds lambda_n, max_{m<=n}X_m>lambda_n i.o. is equivalent to X_n>lambda_n i.o., hence controlled by sum tail probabilities. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.16 pattern: reduce running-maximum events to one-time exceedance events when thresholds increase. Pitfall: The monotonicity lambda_n up to infinity is what prevents one early large value from causing infinitely many later exceedances.

front: Single observations versus maxima under n-normalization (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For iid Y_n, Y_n/n -> 0 a.s. needs E|Y_1|<infinity, max_{m<=n}Y_m/n -> 0 a.s. needs E(Y_1^+)<infinity, and max convergence in probability needs xP(Y_1>x)->0. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.17 pattern: distinguish two-sided single-term control from one-sided running-maximum control. Pitfall: Almost sure and in-probability maxima have different tail requirements.

front: Monotone variance subsequence a.s. upgrade (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For increasing nonnegative X_n with EX_n asymptotic to a n^alpha and variance O(n^beta), beta<2alpha, prove X_n/n^alpha -> a a.s. via sparse subsequences and monotonicity. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.18 pattern: turn variance bounds into a.s. asymptotics when monotonicity bridges gaps. Pitfall: Without monotonicity, subsequence convergence alone would not control intermediate indices.

front: Poisson sums strong law with variable means (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: For independent Poisson X_n with means lambda_n and m_n=sum_{k<=n}lambda_k -> infinity, S_n/m_n -> 1 almost surely. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.19 pattern: use variance summability with the natural cumulative-mean scale. Pitfall: A naive subsequence proof can fail when the means have large jumps; the series proof handles arbitrary lambda_n.

front: St. Petersburg almost-sure limsup explosion (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: In the St. Petersburg game, X_n/(n log_2 n) has infinite limsup almost surely, so the in-probability normalization for sums is not an a.s. law. Exam use: Exercise 2.3.20 pattern: rare giant jumps can destroy almost sure convergence even when weak-law scaling works. Pitfall: Convergence in probability of S_n/(n log n) does not control limsup behavior.

front: Kolmogorov maximal inequality (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: For independent mean-zero variables with finite variances, the probability that partial sums exceed a threshold is bounded by the total variance divided by the threshold squared. Exam use: Main tool for upgrading variance summability to almost sure control of partial sums. Pitfall: The variables must be centered and independent; this is stronger than ordinary Chebyshev for the final sum.

front: Kolmogorov convergence criterion (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: If independent mean-zero variables have summable variances, then their series converges almost surely. Exam use: Use for convergence of centered random series and as a strong-law building block. Pitfall: Summable variances are sufficient, not necessary; do not mistake it for a complete characterization.

front: Fourth-moment strong law shortcut (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: If iid centered variables have finite fourth moment, then S_n/n -> 0 almost surely by summable fourth-moment bounds. Exam use: A quick SLLN proof when high moments are available. Pitfall: This proof is convenient but much stronger than needed; finite first moment is enough for the iid SLLN.

front: IID finite mean strong law (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: If X_i are iid and E|X_1| < infinity with mean mu, then S_n/n -> mu almost surely. Exam use: Central theorem of the chapter and a frequent prelim target. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence is stronger than convergence in probability; the WLLN proof alone is not enough.

front: Strong law normalization habits (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: Strong laws usually show a normalized centered sum tends to zero almost surely, then add back deterministic centering. Exam use: Helps organize proofs for arrays, subsequences, and renewal counting. Pitfall: Centering must be finite; if the mean is infinite, S_n/n behaves differently.

front: Renewal reward ratio via SLLN (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: For cycles with iid working time X_i and downtime Y_i, the long-run working fraction is E X_i/(E X_i+E Y_i). Exam use: Exercise 2.4.1 pattern: renewal reward limits are ratios of mean reward to mean cycle length. Pitfall: Account for the possible incomplete cycle at time t; its contribution is negligible after division by t.

front: Multiplicative recursion becomes additive after logs (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: If a process satisfies |X_n|=product_{k<=n}R_k with iid positive factors and E|log R_1|<infinity, then n^{-1}log|X_n| -> E log R_1 a.s. Exam use: Exercise 2.4.2 pattern: random scaling problems reduce to an SLLN for log radii. Pitfall: Check integrability near zero before applying the SLLN to logarithms.

front: Kelly-style investment growth optimization (2.4 Strong Law of Large Numbers)
back: For fixed portfolio fraction p, long-run log wealth converges to c(p)=E log(ap+(1-p)V); c is concave in p and endpoint derivatives decide whether the optimum is interior. Exam use: Exercise 2.4.3 pattern: optimize almost-sure exponential growth by maximizing expected log return. Pitfall: Maximizing expected wealth and maximizing expected log wealth are different criteria.

front: Kolmogorov three-series theorem (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: For independent variables, convergence of sum X_n is characterized by tail probabilities, truncated means, and truncated variances. Exam use: The main checklist for almost sure convergence of independent random series. Pitfall: The theorem uses a fixed truncation level; changing the cutoff changes the conditions but not the spirit.

front: Large jumps in random series (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: A necessary component for series convergence is that large jumps occur only finitely often, typically sum P(|X_n| > c) < infinity. Exam use: First diagnostic check in random-series problems. Pitfall: Termwise convergence to zero is necessary but not sufficient for series convergence.

front: Truncated centering in random series (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: After removing large jumps, convergence depends on whether the truncated expectations form a convergent deterministic series. Exam use: Prevents hidden deterministic drift from spoiling almost sure convergence. Pitfall: A centered variance argument cannot control a divergent sum of means.

front: Rates for random series tails (2.5.1 Rates of Convergence)
back: Once a random series converges, tail estimates can quantify how fast partial sums approach the limit. Exam use: Useful when a problem asks not just convergence but an almost sure order estimate. Pitfall: A convergence theorem alone may not give the requested rate; track the size of tail variances or probabilities.

front: Infinite mean changes LLN behavior (2.5.2 Infinite Mean)
back: When the mean is infinite or undefined, normalized sums can diverge or be controlled by rare large terms rather than averaging. Exam use: Important for spotting when a finite-mean LLN theorem cannot be applied. Pitfall: Do not center by EX if EX is not finite; the expression may be meaningless.

front: Renewal process basics (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: With iid positive interarrival times xi_i, renewal times T_n = xi_1+...+xi_n and count N(t)=max{n:T_n<=t} form a renewal process. Exam use: Connects laws of large numbers to counting processes and waiting-time problems. Pitfall: Be careful with indexing: some formulas use the last renewal before t and the next renewal after t.

front: Elementary renewal theorem (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: For iid positive interarrival times with finite mean mu, E[N(t)]/t -> 1/mu. Exam use: Useful for expected counts in long-run systems. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence of N(t)/t and convergence of its expectation are related but require different arguments.

front: Renewal equation (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: Many renewal quantities satisfy H(t)=h(t)+ integral_0^t H(t-s)dF(s), where F is the interarrival distribution. Exam use: Primary modeling move for renewal reward, age, residual lifetime, and alternating renewal problems. Pitfall: The input function h and the renewal function must match the same convention for time zero.

front: Key renewal theorem (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: For nonarithmetic renewals and directly Riemann integrable h, renewal convolutions converge to (1/mu) integral h. Exam use: Use for limiting age, residual lifetime, and renewal reward calculations. Pitfall: Arithmetic interarrival distributions have lattice oscillations; the nonarithmetic hypothesis matters.

front: Superadditivity for large-deviation probabilities (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: For iid sums, pi_n=P(S_n>=na) satisfies pi_{m+n} >= pi_m pi_n, so log pi_n is superadditive and n^{-1}log pi_n has a limit. Exam use: Establishes existence of the exponential decay rate before computing it. Pitfall: The limit can be zero or negative infinity; existence alone does not guarantee a useful exponential bound.

front: Chernoff exponential bound (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: If phi(theta)=E exp(theta X) is finite, then P(S_n>=na) <= exp(-n(a theta - log phi(theta))). Exam use: Main upper-bound engine for large-deviation estimates. Pitfall: Optimize over theta>0; a random unoptimized theta may prove decay but not the sharp rate.

front: Exponential tilting (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: The tilted law F_theta has density proportional to exp(theta x) relative to F, and its mean is phi'(theta)/phi(theta). Exam use: Used for the lower bound in Cramer-style large deviations by making the rare event typical. Pitfall: Tilting is only valid where the moment generating function is finite.

front: Cramer rate formula in the smooth case (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: When a>mu and there is theta_a with a=phi'(theta_a)/phi(theta_a), n^{-1}log P(S_n>=na) -> -a theta_a + log phi(theta_a). Exam use: Compute sharp exponential rates for normal, exponential, Bernoulli, and Poisson examples. Pitfall: Boundary cases require separate treatment when the optimizing theta is not inside the finite-mgf interval.

front: Subsequence control upgraded to full control (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: A sparse almost-sure estimate can be upgraded to all times when the gaps are controlled by monotonicity or a maximal inequality. Exam use: Exercise 2.5.1 pattern: prove a rate on n_m=floor(m^alpha), then bridge the gaps. Pitfall: Subsequence convergence alone is insufficient; the gap-control step carries the proof.

front: Two-series variance criterion (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: Independent mean-zero variables with summable variances have an almost surely convergent series. Exam use: Quickly solves centered series such as sum X_n sin(n pi t)/n. Pitfall: The variables must be independent and centered; otherwise deterministic drift must be treated separately.

front: Independent series variance test (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: For independent centered summands, finite total variance is enough for almost-sure convergence. Exam use: Exercise 2.5.3 pattern: trigonometric factors are harmless when bounded by 1. Pitfall: Do not require identical distributions; only independence, centering, and summable variances.

front: Kronecker lemma for normalized sums (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: If sum x_n/n converges, then n^{-1} sum_{k<=n} x_k -> 0. Exam use: Turns convergence of a random series into a strong law for averages. Pitfall: The weights must increase to infinity; here the natural weight is n.

front: Tail-sum moment test (2.2 Weak Laws of Large Numbers)
back: For p>0, E|X|^p is finite exactly when the tail probabilities P(|X|^p>n) are summable up to constants. Exam use: Exercise 2.5.2 pattern: a.s. term control plus Borel-Cantelli forces finite moments. Pitfall: The tail variable is |X|^p, not |X|; keep the normalization consistent.

front: Random power-series radius (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: The radius of sum X_n z^n is 1/limsup |X_n|^{1/n}; for iid coefficients this is decided by E log^+ |X_1|. Exam use: Exercise 2.5.8 pattern: logarithmic tails determine random analytic radius. Pitfall: Nonzero coefficients must occur infinitely often to rule out a larger radius.

front: Levy maximal inequality for independent increments (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: A maximum of partial increments is controlled by the terminal increment times a lower bound on remaining-tail probabilities. Exam use: Core tool for Levy's theorem and convergence-in-probability to almost-sure convergence upgrades. Pitfall: The independence is between the first-crossing event and the future tail increment.

front: Independent increments in partial sums (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: For sums of independent variables, disjoint blocks of increments are independent. Exam use: Used inside first-crossing arguments and block decompositions. Pitfall: The original variables must be independent; identical distribution is not needed.

front: Cauchy criterion in probability (2.5 Convergence of Random Series)
back: Convergence in probability implies the sequence is Cauchy in probability, uniformly over sufficiently far tails. Exam use: Starting point for proving Levy's theorem. Pitfall: Cauchy in probability alone does not immediately give a.s. convergence without extra structure.

front: Convergence in probability (2.2 Weak Laws of Large Numbers)
back: X_n -> X in probability means P(|X_n-X|>epsilon)->0 for every epsilon>0. Exam use: Used throughout weak laws, maximal estimates, and large-deviation preliminaries. Pitfall: It is weaker than almost-sure convergence and does not by itself control all subsequences pathwise.

front: Chebyshev inequality for sums (2.2 Weak Laws of Large Numbers)
back: If a centered sum has finite variance, P(|S|>x) <= Var(S)/x^2. Exam use: Exercise 2.5.12 pattern: dyadic Chebyshev bounds become summable with a logarithmic correction. Pitfall: Chebyshev gives polynomial bounds; a log factor may be needed for summability.

front: Renewal function (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: The renewal function U(t)=E N_t records the expected number of renewals by time t. Exam use: Exercise 2.6.1 pattern: bound U(t) using E(xi_1 wedge t). Pitfall: Indexing conventions for N_t vary; keep the same convention through the proof.

front: Wald identity with truncated interarrivals (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: For bounded truncated increments xi_i wedge t and a renewal stopping time, the expected stopped sum factors as E increment times E count. Exam use: Useful for elementary renewal estimates without assuming second moments. Pitfall: Do not apply untruncated Wald blindly when the stopping time or mean may be problematic.

front: Renewal counting strong law (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: If interarrival times have finite mean mu, then N(t)/t -> 1/mu almost surely. Exam use: Converts cycle averages into long-run time rates. Pitfall: The inversion requires positive interarrival times and finite mean.

front: Uniform integrability from L2 bounds (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: A family bounded in L2 is uniformly integrable, so a.s. convergence plus the L2 bound gives convergence of expectations. Exam use: Exercise 2.6.2 pattern: upgrade N(t)/t -> 1/mu a.s. to U(t)/t -> 1/mu. Pitfall: Almost-sure convergence alone does not imply convergence of expectations.

front: Poisson memoryless property (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: After a stopping time independent of future arrivals, the waiting time to the next Poisson arrival is exponential and independent of the past. Exam use: Turns accepted arrivals after service completion into a renewal process. Pitfall: This finite-time memoryless identity is special to exponential interarrivals.

front: Renewal reward theorem (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: Long-run reward per unit time equals expected reward per cycle divided by expected cycle length. Exam use: Use for server acceptance fractions, machine uptime, and alternating renewal rewards. Pitfall: Check the cycle definition: rewards and lengths must correspond to the same cycles.

front: Age and residual lifetime limits (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: The limiting tail for age, residual life, or their joint event is an integrated tail of the interarrival distribution divided by mu. Exam use: Exercises 2.6.4 and 2.6.6 pattern: identify the renewal interval straddling t. Pitfall: The age and residual life are not generally independent in the limit.

front: Alternating renewal process (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: A system alternates between on- and off-periods; the long-run on probability is mean on-time divided by mean cycle length. Exam use: Exercise 2.6.7 pattern: machine availability equals mu_on/(mu_on+mu_off). Pitfall: The cycle law is the convolution of one on-period and one off-period.

front: Renewal density (2.6 Renewal Theory)
back: If F has density f, the non-initial renewal measure has density v=sum_{n>=1} f^{*n}, satisfying v=f+v*F. Exam use: Exercise 2.6.9 pattern: apply the key renewal theorem to f to get v(t)->1/mu. Pitfall: Direct Riemann integrability of f is the hypothesis that permits the limit theorem.

front: Large-deviation exponent gamma (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: gamma(a) is the limiting exponential rate n^{-1} log P(S_n >= na), possibly 0 or -infinity. Exam use: Exercises 2.7.1 and 2.7.2 pattern: analyze the effective domain and concavity of gamma. Pitfall: A rate of -infinity corresponds to an impossible event, not merely a very small probability.

front: Independent sums factor rare-event probabilities (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: For iid sums, events involving disjoint blocks can be multiplied to get lower bounds on large-deviation probabilities. Exam use: Useful for superadditivity and concavity of large-deviation rates. Pitfall: The block thresholds must add to the desired total threshold.

front: Superadditivity lemma (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: If a_{m+n} >= a_m+a_n, then a_n/n has a limit equal to its supremum in the extended real sense. Exam use: Used to prove existence of gamma(a). Pitfall: Signs matter: log probabilities are superadditive here, not subadditive.

front: Concavity from block mixing (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: Mixing blocks with thresholds a and b proves gamma(lambda a+(1-lambda)b) >= lambda gamma(a)+(1-lambda)gamma(b). Exam use: Exercise 2.7.2 pattern: concavity implies local Lipschitz regularity on the finite domain. Pitfall: The extension from rational to real lambda needs monotonicity or another continuity argument.

front: Poisson large-deviation rate (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: For iid Poisson(1), P(S_n>=na) has exponent -a log a+a-1 for a>1. Exam use: Exercise 2.7.3 pattern: compute Cramer rates by solving tilted mean equals a. Pitfall: The displayed formula is for upper tails a>1.

front: Moment-generating-function upper bounds (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: Simple analytic inequalities for phi(theta) lead to usable Chernoff bounds even when the exact optimizer is not used. Exam use: Exercise 2.7.4 pattern: cosh(theta)-1 <= beta theta^2 for theta<=1. Pitfall: The bound is only valid over the range where the analytic inequality was proved.

front: Subgaussian bound for fair coin sums (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: For Rademacher sums, bounding cosh(theta) by exp(beta theta^2) gives a Gaussian-type upper tail. Exam use: Gives a clean exponential estimate P(S_n>=an) <= exp(-n a^2/(4 beta)) for 0<=a<=1. Pitfall: This is a valid bound but not the sharp Cramer exponent.

front: No positive mgf gives zero exponential rate (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: If all positive exponential moments are infinite, upper large deviations cannot have a strictly negative exponential rate. Exam use: Exercise 2.7.5 pattern: heavy tails make large deviations subexponential on the logarithmic n-scale. Pitfall: This conclusion concerns exponential rate only; probabilities may still tend to zero.

front: One big jump lower bound (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: For integrable mean-zero iid sums, P(S_n>=na) is at least asymptotically n P(X_1>=n(a+epsilon)). Exam use: Exercise 2.7.6 pattern: lower-bound heavy-tail large deviations by a single summand. Pitfall: Integrability is used to ensure n P(X_1>cn)->0 and to apply the weak law.

front: Weak law of large numbers (2.2 Weak Laws of Large Numbers)
back: For iid integrable variables with mean mu, S_n/n -> mu in probability. Exam use: Supports probability estimates for the contribution of all non-extreme summands. Pitfall: Weak convergence controls high-probability behavior, not pathwise eventual behavior.

front: Tail probability normalization (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: Compare rare-event probabilities to n times a single-tail probability when one large summand drives the event. Exam use: Useful in heavy-tail large-deviation lower bounds. Pitfall: The denominator must match the same threshold scale as the constructed big jump.

front: Borel-Cantelli lemmas (2.3 Borel-Cantelli Lemmas)
back: Summable event probabilities imply only finitely many occurrences; for independent events, divergent probability sums imply infinitely many occurrences. Exam use: A general retrieval alias for exercises that use either Borel-Cantelli direction. Pitfall: The divergent direction requires independence or a suitable substitute.

front: Cramer theorem (2.7 Large Deviations)
back: For light-tailed iid sums, the upper-tail exponential decay rate is the negative Legendre transform of log E exp(theta X). Exam use: A general retrieval alias for Cramer-rate computations such as the Poisson example. Pitfall: The theorem requires finiteness of the mgf near the optimizing tilt.

front: Stirling local binomial approximation (3.1 The De Moivre-Laplace Theorem)
back: For a simple symmetric walk, P(S_{2n}=2k) is asymptotic to (pi n)^(-1/2) exp(-x^2/2) when 2k/sqrt(2n) tends to x. Exam use: Use as the prototype local normal approximation before invoking characteristic functions. Pitfall: The approximation is local and depends on the lattice parity of S_{2n}.

front: De Moivre-Laplace theorem (3.1 The De Moivre-Laplace Theorem)
back: For a simple symmetric random walk, S_m/sqrt(m) converges in distribution to the standard normal law. Exam use: A concrete CLT model for coin flips, binomial normal approximation, and continuity-correction intuition. Pitfall: Do not forget the even/odd lattice issue; the theorem for all m follows after a small parity argument.

front: Product exponential limit (3.1 The De Moivre-Laplace Theorem)
back: If max_j |c_{j,n}| goes to 0, sum_j c_{j,n} tends to lambda, and the sums of absolute values are bounded, then product_j(1+c_{j,n}) tends to exp(lambda). Exam use: Fast route for birthday, occupancy, and rare-event product limits. Pitfall: The maximum term condition is essential; a single large factor can break the exponential approximation.

front: Stirling large-deviation patterns (3.1 The De Moivre-Laplace Theorem)
back: Stirling's formula also gives exponential rates for binomial and Poisson tails at linear deviations from the mean. Exam use: Useful when an exam asks for n^{-1} log P(S_n >= n a) before the full large-deviation theorem is available. Pitfall: Normal approximation is not valid on linear-deviation scales; use exponential rates instead.

front: Weak convergence definition (3.2 Weak Convergence)
back: Distribution functions F_n converge weakly to F when F_n(x) tends to F(x) at every continuity point of F; random variables converge in distribution when their laws do. Exam use: This is the language used in CLT, Poisson approximation, stable limits, and multivariate limits. Pitfall: Convergence at discontinuity points of the limit is not required and can fail even for X+1/n tending to X.

front: Waiting for rare events gives an exponential limit (3.2.1 Examples)
back: If X_p is geometric with success probability p, then p X_p converges in distribution to an exponential(1) random variable as p tends to 0. Exam use: Recognize geometric waiting times under rare-event scaling. Pitfall: The scale is p X_p, not X_p/p; the waiting time itself diverges.

front: Birthday collision Rayleigh limit (3.2.1 Examples)
back: For iid uniform draws from N boxes, the first collision time T_N satisfies P(T_N/sqrt(N)>x) tending to exp(-x^2/2). Exam use: A standard occupancy/collision scaling pattern. Pitfall: The natural scale is sqrt(N), not N, because collisions depend on pairs.

front: Scheffe theorem for densities (3.2.1 Examples)
back: If probability densities f_n converge pointwise to a density f, then the corresponding probability measures converge in total variation, hence weakly. Exam use: Use when a limit distribution is easiest to prove through densities, such as central order statistics. Pitfall: Pointwise convergence of densities is stronger than weak convergence and is not necessary.

front: Quantile representation for weak convergence (3.2.2 Theory)
back: If F_n converges weakly to F, one can construct random variables with these laws on (0,1) that converge almost surely. Exam use: Lets proof problems transfer weak convergence into almost sure convergence for bounded continuous mappings. Pitfall: The constructed variables need not be the original variables; this is a representation argument.

front: Bounded continuous test function characterization (3.2.2 Theory)
back: X_n converges in distribution to X iff E g(X_n) tends to E g(X) for every bounded continuous function g. Exam use: The default way to define weak convergence on general topological spaces. Pitfall: Boundedness matters; unbounded continuous functions need extra tightness or uniform integrability.

front: Continuous mapping theorem (3.2.2 Theory)
back: If X_n converges in distribution to X and g is measurable with P(X in D_g)=0, then g(X_n) converges in distribution to g(X). Exam use: Use for Slutsky-type transformations, maxima maps, ratios away from zero, and normalization changes. Pitfall: A discontinuity with positive limit probability can invalidate the conclusion.

front: Portmanteau open-closed criteria (3.2.2 Theory)
back: Weak convergence is equivalent to lower bounds on open sets, upper bounds on closed sets, and convergence on Borel sets whose boundary has limit probability zero. Exam use: Use when events are not intervals but have negligible boundary under the limit. Pitfall: The direction of the inequalities is easy to reverse; open sets get liminf lower bounds and closed sets get limsup upper bounds.

front: Helly selection and tightness (3.2.2 Theory)
back: Every sequence of distribution functions has a vaguely convergent subsequence; subsequential limits are probability laws exactly when mass is tight. Exam use: Core compactness tool for proving existence of subsequential distributional limits. Pitfall: Vague subsequential limits can lose mass, so Helly alone is not enough for weak convergence.

front: Moment tightness criterion (3.2.2 Theory)
back: If sup_n E phi(X_n) is finite for some nonnegative phi with phi(x) tending to infinity as |x| tends to infinity, then the laws of X_n are tight. Exam use: Fast tightness check using bounded moments or coercive Lyapunov functions. Pitfall: A bounded first or second moment gives tightness, but tightness does not require moments.

front: Subsequence principle for weak convergence (3.2.2 Theory)
back: If every subsequence has a further subsequence converging weakly to the same law, then the full sequence converges weakly to that law. Exam use: Useful for proving convergence after compactness/tightness arguments. Pitfall: The further subsequential limit must be uniquely identified; tightness only gives candidates.

front: Convergence in probability implies weak convergence (3.2 Exercises and Consequences)
back: If X_n tends to X in probability, then X_n converges in distribution to X; if X_n converges in distribution to a constant c, then X_n tends to c in probability. Exam use: Often used to replace negligible error terms in limit theorems. Pitfall: Weak convergence to a nonconstant random variable does not imply convergence in probability on the original space.

front: Converging together and Slutsky patterns (3.2 Exercises and Consequences)
back: If X_n converges in distribution to X and Y_n converges in distribution to a constant c, then X_n+Y_n, X_n Y_n, and X_n/Y_n (c not 0) converge to the corresponding transformed limits. Exam use: Indispensable for replacing random normalizers by deterministic limits. Pitfall: The denominator case needs the limit constant away from zero.

front: Characteristic function basics (3.3.1 Definition, Inversion Formula)
back: The characteristic function of X is phi(t)=E exp(i t X); it is uniformly continuous, positive definite, satisfies phi(0)=1, and determines the distribution. Exam use: Main transform language for CLT and distributional convergence. Pitfall: A pointwise limit of characteristic functions is a characteristic function only if it is continuous at zero.

front: Characteristic functions multiply for independent sums (3.3.1 Definition, Inversion Formula)
back: If X and Y are independent, the characteristic function of X+Y is phi_X(t) phi_Y(t). Exam use: Turns sums of independent random variables into products and powers of transforms. Pitfall: The product rule requires independence; uncorrelated variables are not enough.

front: Standard characteristic function table (3.3.1 Definition, Inversion Formula)
back: Coin flip: cos t; Poisson(lambda): exp(lambda(e^{it}-1)); normal(mu,sigma^2): exp(i mu t - sigma^2 t^2/2); uniform(a,b): (e^{itb}-e^{ita})/(it(b-a)); exponential(lambda): lambda/(lambda-it). Exam use: Recognition table for prelim transform calculations. Pitfall: Check parameter conventions carefully, especially normal variance and exponential rate versus mean.

front: Characteristic function inversion formula (3.3.1 Definition, Inversion Formula)
back: A distribution can be recovered from its characteristic function by integrating (exp(-ita)-exp(-itb))/(it) against phi(t) over symmetric intervals and taking limits at continuity endpoints. Exam use: Use to prove uniqueness and to identify distributional limits from transforms. Pitfall: Endpoint atoms require care; inversion is cleanest for intervals whose endpoints have zero mass.

front: Integrable characteristic function gives bounded density (3.3.1 Definition, Inversion Formula)
back: If a characteristic function is integrable, the distribution has a bounded continuous density given by Fourier inversion. Exam use: Shows stable or limiting laws have densities when their characteristic functions are integrable. Pitfall: Integrability of phi is sufficient, not necessary, for having a density.

front: Levy continuity theorem (3.3.2 Weak Convergence)
back: Probability measures converge weakly iff their characteristic functions converge pointwise to a function continuous at zero, in which case the limit is the characteristic function of the weak limit. Exam use: The main engine for proving CLT, Poisson limits, stable limits, and multivariate limit theorems. Pitfall: Pointwise convergence to a discontinuous-at-zero function does not define a probability limit.

front: Moments from characteristic function derivatives (3.3.3 Moments and Derivatives)
back: If E|X|^n is finite, then phi has n derivatives and phi^{(k)}(0)=i^k E X^k for k up to n. Exam use: Compute moments and build Taylor expansions for CLT proofs. Pitfall: Existence of a few derivatives at zero can be subtler than finite moments unless the theorem's hypotheses are met.

front: Second-order characteristic function expansion (3.3.3 Moments and Derivatives)
