back: If EX=0 and EX^2=sigma^2 is finite, then phi(t)=1-sigma^2 t^2/2+o(t^2) as t tends to zero. Exam use: This is the one-line input behind the iid CLT by characteristic functions. Pitfall: The expansion is local near zero; do not apply it at fixed nonzero t without scaling.

front: Polya criterion for characteristic functions (3.3.4 Polya's Criterion)
back: A real, nonnegative, even, convex-on-positive-rays function with phi(0)=1 and phi(t) tending to 0 at infinity is a characteristic function. Exam use: Useful for constructing stable-law examples such as exp(-|t|^alpha) for 0<alpha<2. Pitfall: The criterion is sufficient, not necessary; many characteristic functions are not nonnegative or convex.

front: Moment problem and Carleman-type uniqueness (3.3.5 The Moment Problem)
back: Under suitable growth conditions on even moments, a distribution is determined by its moments. Exam use: Helps decide when matching all moments identifies a law. Pitfall: Moment equality alone is not always enough; lognormal-type examples show nonuniqueness can occur.

front: IID central limit theorem (3.4.1 i.i.d. Sequences)
back: If X_i are iid with mean mu and variance sigma^2 in (0,infinity), then (S_n-n mu)/(sigma sqrt(n)) converges in distribution to standard normal. Exam use: The central theorem for normal approximations and asymptotic distribution calculations. Pitfall: Finite nonzero variance is required for this form; infinite variance can lead to nonnormal stable limits.

front: Normal approximation and continuity correction (3.4.1 i.i.d. Sequences)
back: For binomial or lattice sums, approximate probabilities by normal intervals and shift interval endpoints by half a lattice step for better accuracy. Exam use: Useful for coin-flip, dice, roulette, binomial, and Poisson approximations. Pitfall: The CLT gives large-n accuracy; small tails and lattice endpoints may need correction or sharper bounds.

front: Lindeberg-Feller central limit theorem (3.4.2 Triangular Arrays)
back: For independent mean-zero triangular arrays with total variance tending to 1, the row sum converges to standard normal iff the Lindeberg condition holds. Exam use: The main CLT for non-identically distributed arrays. Pitfall: The condition is row-wise; do not replace it by iid assumptions unless the array is built that way.

front: Lyapunov condition implies Lindeberg (3.4.2 Triangular Arrays)
back: If a triangular array has normalized (2+delta)-moments whose row sum tends to zero, then the Lindeberg condition holds. Exam use: Quick sufficient check for triangular-array CLTs. Pitfall: Lyapunov is stronger than Lindeberg; failure of Lyapunov does not rule out a CLT.

front: Random index CLT (3.4.1 i.i.d. Sequences)
back: If N_t/a(t) tends to 1 in probability and iid centered finite-variance sums satisfy the CLT, then S_{N_t}/sqrt(a(t)) has the same normal limit. Exam use: Useful for renewal CLTs and random-sample-size problems. Pitfall: The random index must be close on the scale needed by the partial-sum fluctuations.

front: Erdos-Kac central limit theorem (3.4.3 Prime Divisors)
back: The number of distinct prime divisors of a uniformly chosen integer up to n, after centering and scaling by log log n, converges to the standard normal law. Exam use: A landmark example where arithmetic statistics obey a CLT. Pitfall: The variance scale is log log n, not log n.

front: Berry-Esseen rate (3.4.4 Rates of Convergence)
back: For iid centered variables with finite third absolute moment and variance one, the normal approximation error in Kolmogorov distance is O(E|X|^3/sqrt(n)). Exam use: Use when a problem asks for an explicit CLT error rate. Pitfall: Finite variance alone gives convergence but not this rate.

front: Lattice span from characteristic functions (3.5 Local Limit Theorems)
back: For a lattice distribution, the set of t with |phi(t)|=1 encodes the lattice spacing; otherwise |phi(t)|<1 away from zero on compact intervals. Exam use: Key diagnostic for local limit theorems. Pitfall: Local limits differ in lattice and nonlattice cases; the span matters.

front: Local limit theorem (3.5 Local Limit Theorems)
back: Under suitable lattice hypotheses and finite variance, point probabilities of centered sums are approximated by the normal density times the lattice spacing over sqrt(n). Exam use: Use when approximating exact probabilities, not just cumulative probabilities. Pitfall: The ordinary CLT does not imply local probability estimates by itself.

front: Poisson convergence for rare Bernoulli arrays (3.6.1 The Basic Limit Theorem)
back: For independent Bernoulli variables in row n, if the maximum success probability tends to zero and the sum of success probabilities tends to lambda, then the row sum converges to Poisson(lambda). Exam use: The standard law of small numbers for rare-event counts. Pitfall: The largest individual probability must vanish; total mean convergence alone is not enough.

front: Total variation Poisson approximation (3.6.1 The Basic Limit Theorem)
back: A sum of independent Bernoulli variables is close in total variation to a Poisson variable with matching mean when the sum of squared success probabilities is small. Exam use: Gives quantitative Poisson approximation for rare counts. Pitfall: Total variation is stronger than weak convergence; the error bound depends on small individual probabilities.

front: Matching fixed points converge to Poisson (3.6.2 Two Examples with Dependence)
back: The number of fixed points in a uniform random permutation converges to Poisson(1). Exam use: Classic dependent rare-event Poisson limit. Pitfall: The indicators are not independent, so the basic Bernoulli-array theorem does not apply directly.

front: Occupancy empty-box Poisson limit (3.6.2 Two Examples with Dependence)
back: When r balls are thrown into n boxes and n exp(-r/n) tends to lambda, the number of empty boxes converges to Poisson(lambda). Exam use: Appears in coupon collector and hashing problems. Pitfall: Empty-box events are dependent; independence heuristics need justification.

front: Poisson process from independent increments (3.7 Poisson Processes)
back: A counting process with stationary independent increments, no multiple jumps in small intervals, and jump probability asymptotic to lambda h has N(0,t) distributed as Poisson(lambda t). Exam use: Foundation for continuous-time rare-event models. Pitfall: The small-interval multiple-jump condition is needed to reduce counts to Bernoulli indicators.

front: Compound Poisson process (3.7.1 Compound Poisson Processes)
back: If N is a Poisson process and Y_i are iid jumps independent of N, then sum_{i<=N(t)} Y_i is a compound Poisson process with characteristic function exp(lambda t (E exp(i theta Y)-1)). Exam use: Models random sums and jump processes with nonunit jump sizes. Pitfall: Do not confuse the rate of arrivals with the distribution of jump sizes.

front: Thinning of a Poisson process (3.7.2 Thinning)
back: If each point of a rate lambda Poisson process is independently assigned a type j with probability p_j, then the type-specific counting processes are independent Poisson processes with rates lambda p_j. Exam use: Use for marked processes, occupancy via Poissonization, and splitting arrivals into classes. Pitfall: Independence of the marks is essential for independent thinned processes.

front: Poisson arrivals conditional on count are order statistics (3.7.3 Conditioning)
back: Given N(t)=n in a homogeneous Poisson process, the arrival times in [0,t] have the same joint distribution as the order statistics of n iid uniform(0,t) variables. Exam use: Turns Poisson-process conditioning questions into uniform order-statistic problems. Pitfall: This is conditional on the count; unconditionally the arrival times are not a fixed-size sample.

front: Stable law domain of attraction (3.8 Stable Laws)
back: Heavy-tailed iid sums with regularly varying tails of index alpha in (0,2) converge after n^{1/alpha}-type scaling and suitable centering to an alpha-stable law. Exam use: Explains nonnormal limits when variance is infinite. Pitfall: Centering depends on alpha and tail balance; do not reuse finite-variance CLT normalization.

front: Stable characteristic exponent (3.8 Stable Laws)
back: Stable laws have characteristic functions with exponent governed by alpha, scale, skewness, and centering parameters; symmetric stable laws have exp(-c |t|^alpha). Exam use: Recognition tool for identifying stable limits from characteristic functions. Pitfall: The alpha=1 case has special logarithmic centering behavior in nonsymmetric cases.

front: Convergence of types theorem (3.8 Stable Laws)
back: If a_n X_n+b_n converges to a nondegenerate limit and c_n X_n+d_n converges to another nondegenerate limit, then c_n/a_n and d_n-b_n c_n/a_n have limits linking the two laws by affine transformation. Exam use: Useful for proving uniqueness of scaling and centering in stable-limit problems. Pitfall: The limit must be nondegenerate; constants allow pathological normalization changes.

front: Infinitely divisible distributions (3.9 Infinitely Divisible Distributions)
back: A distribution is infinitely divisible if for every n it can be represented as the law of a sum of n iid random variables. Exam use: Normal, Poisson, compound Poisson, and stable laws are key examples. Pitfall: Not every distribution is infinitely divisible; bounded nonconstant laws are not.

front: Levy-Khinchin representation (3.9 Infinitely Divisible Distributions)
back: Characteristic functions of infinitely divisible laws have an exponent decomposed into drift, Gaussian variance, and an integral term governed by a Levy measure. Exam use: The master formula unifying normal, Poisson, compound Poisson, and stable laws. Pitfall: The Levy measure is not a probability measure; it controls jump intensity and must satisfy an integrability condition near zero.

front: Multivariate weak convergence (3.10 Limit Theorems in R^d)
back: Weak convergence in R^d can be defined through bounded continuous test functions, rectangle distribution functions at continuity points, or probability convergence on continuity sets. Exam use: Needed for joint convergence, random vectors, and multivariate CLT. Pitfall: Coordinatewise convergence alone does not imply joint convergence unless dependence is controlled.

front: Multivariate characteristic function convergence (3.10 Limit Theorems in R^d)
back: Random vectors converge in distribution iff their multivariate characteristic functions converge pointwise to a function continuous at the origin. Exam use: Primary method for proving joint limit theorems. Pitfall: Checking only marginal characteristic functions misses dependence; the full vector transform is needed.

front: Cramer-Wold device (3.10 Limit Theorems in R^d)
back: A sufficient and standard condition for X_n in R^d to converge to X is that theta dot X_n converges in distribution to theta dot X for every theta in R^d. Exam use: Turns multivariate convergence into one-dimensional convergence problems. Pitfall: All directions theta are needed; finitely many coordinate projections are not enough.

front: Central limit theorem in R^d (3.10 Limit Theorems in R^d)
back: For iid random vectors with mean vector m and covariance matrix Sigma, n^{-1/2} sum (X_i-m) converges to a multivariate normal with covariance Sigma. Exam use: Use for joint asymptotic normality and random-walk scaling in higher dimensions. Pitfall: A singular covariance matrix still gives a valid degenerate multivariate normal limit.

front: Conditional expectation as Radon-Nikodym projection (4.1 Conditional Expectation)
back: E(X|F) is the F-measurable integrable random variable Y satisfying integral_A Y dP = integral_A X dP for every A in F. Exam use: The starting point for every martingale verification and every conditioning calculation. Pitfall: Conditional expectations are only unique up to almost sure equality, so pointwise formulas need version awareness.

front: Uniqueness and locality of conditional expectation (4.1 Conditional Expectation)
back: Versions of E(X|F) agree almost surely; if X1=X2 on B in F, then their conditional expectations agree on B almost surely. Exam use: Use locality to compute conditional expectations piecewise on atoms or events known at the conditioning time. Pitfall: The event where the variables agree must belong to the conditioning sigma-field.

front: Conditional expectation on a countable partition (4.1.1 Examples)
back: If F is generated by a countable positive-probability partition, E(X|F) is constant on each atom and equals the average of X over that atom. Exam use: Turns abstract conditioning into the familiar finite-state formula used in Bayes and Markov-chain problems. Pitfall: Atoms with probability zero cannot be handled by dividing by P(atom).

front: Conditional density formula (4.1.1 Examples)
back: For a joint density f(x,y), E(g(X)|Y)=h(Y), where h(y) is the conditional-density average of g against f(x,y). Exam use: Use for regression-style computations and conditional distribution questions with joint densities. Pitfall: Conditioning on Y=y is a density formula, not literal conditioning on a positive-probability event.

front: Conditioning with independent variables (4.1.1 Examples)
back: If X and Y are independent and g(x)=E phi(x,Y), then E(phi(X,Y)|X)=g(X). Exam use: A quick way to integrate out fresh independent noise while leaving the present state fixed. Pitfall: Do not replace Y by its mean unless phi is linear in Y.

front: Linearity, monotonicity, and monotone convergence conditionally (4.1.2 Properties)
back: Conditional expectation is linear, preserves order, and respects monotone limits under the same integrability hypotheses as ordinary expectation. Exam use: These are the algebra rules behind almost every martingale manipulation. Pitfall: Check integrability hypotheses before using linearity with signed variables.

front: Conditional Jensen and Lp contraction (4.1.2 Properties)
back: For convex phi, phi(E(X|F)) <= E(phi(X)|F); in particular conditional expectation is a contraction on Lp for p >= 1. Exam use: Use to prove submartingale transforms and uniform Lp bounds after conditioning. Pitfall: The inequality is almost sure, and the convex function must be integrable after composition.

front: Tower property: the smaller sigma-field wins (4.1.2 Properties)
back: If F1 subset F2, then E(E(X|F1)|F2)=E(X|F1) and E(E(X|F2)|F1)=E(X|F1). Exam use: Essential for collapsing repeated conditionings in martingale and Markov arguments. Pitfall: The order of unrelated sigma-fields cannot be swapped; nesting is the whole point.

front: Taking out what is known (4.1.2 Properties)
back: If X is F-measurable and the products are integrable, then E(XY|F)=X E(Y|F). Exam use: Lets predictable factors pass through conditional expectation in martingale increment calculations. Pitfall: The multiplier must be measurable with respect to the conditioning sigma-field.

front: Conditional expectation as L2 best predictor (4.1.2 Properties)
back: When EX^2 is finite, E(X|F) is the F-measurable square-integrable variable minimizing E(X-Y)^2. Exam use: Useful for variance decompositions and interpreting conditional expectation as optimal prediction. Pitfall: The projection picture needs second moments; L1 conditional expectation exists more generally.

front: Regular conditional distributions (4.1.3 Regular Conditional Probabilities*)
back: A regular conditional distribution gives a probability kernel whose A-coordinate versions equal P(X in A|G). Exam use: Use when a problem wants conditioning as a genuine conditional law rather than one function at a time. Pitfall: Regular conditional probabilities need not exist on arbitrary measurable spaces.

front: Martingale, submartingale, and supermartingale definitions (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: An adapted integrable process is a martingale when E(X_{n+1}|F_n)=X_n; replacing equality by >= or <= gives submartingales or supermartingales. Exam use: Every martingale problem starts by identifying the filtration, integrability, adaptedness, and the one-step conditional expectation. Pitfall: The filtration is part of the data; a process can be a martingale for one filtration and not another.

front: Linear, quadratic, and exponential random-walk martingales (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: For iid increments, S_n-n mu, S_n^2-n sigma^2 in the centered finite-variance case, and exp(theta S_n)/phi(theta)^n are martingales under their natural hypotheses. Exam use: These are the main engines for gambler's ruin, hitting probabilities, Wald identities, and exponential tail bounds. Pitfall: The exponential martingale requires the moment generating function to be finite at the chosen theta.

front: Multi-step conditional expectation property (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: For n>m, martingales satisfy E(X_n|F_m)=X_m; submartingales give >= and supermartingales give <=. Exam use: Use whenever a future stopped or terminal value is related back to present information. Pitfall: The direction of the inequality reverses between submartingales and supermartingales.

front: Convex transforms make submartingales (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: A convex function of a martingale is a submartingale; an increasing convex function of a submartingale is also a submartingale. Exam use: Build nonnegative submartingales such as |X_n|, X_n^2, and exp(theta X_n) for maximal inequalities. Pitfall: The transformed variables must be integrable.

front: Martingale transforms and no profitable gambling systems (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: If H_n is nonnegative predictable and X is a supermartingale, then the gains process sum H_m(X_m-X_{m-1}) is a supermartingale. Exam use: Formalizes optional betting strategies and proves stopped supermartingales remain supermartingales. Pitfall: Predictability is essential; stakes cannot depend on the next outcome.

front: Stopped processes preserve martingale type (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: If N is a stopping time and X is a supermartingale, then X_{N wedge n} is a supermartingale; analogous statements hold for martingales and submartingales. Exam use: Use before applying convergence or inequality theorems to hitting-time stopped processes. Pitfall: Stopping alone does not guarantee equality of expectations at unbounded times.

front: Doob upcrossing inequality (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: For a submartingale, the expected number of upcrossings of [a,b] up to n is bounded by E(X_n-a)^+/(b-a). Exam use: The key tool for proving almost sure convergence from bounded positive expectations. Pitfall: The interval is [a,b] with a<b; upcrossings are completed crossings, not mere visits.

front: Submartingale almost sure convergence theorem (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: If X_n is a submartingale with sup_n E X_n^+ finite, then X_n converges almost surely to a finite limit. Exam use: A default convergence theorem for bounded or nonnegative martingales after choosing the right sign. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence does not automatically imply L1 convergence or preservation of expectations.

front: Nonnegative supermartingale convergence (4.2 Martingales, Almost Sure Convergence)
back: Every nonnegative supermartingale converges almost surely to a finite limit, with finite expected limit bounded by the initial expectation. Exam use: Extremely useful for likelihood ratios, branching process normalizations, and stopped random walks. Pitfall: The limit can have smaller expectation than X_0; equality needs uniform integrability or stronger optional stopping conditions.

front: Bounded-increment convergence criterion (4.3.1 Bounded Increments)
back: A supermartingale with bounded increments cannot keep drifting on a bounded interval without converging; stopping and shifting make it nonnegative. Exam use: Use for random walks or games where increments are controlled but the process is not globally nonnegative. Pitfall: You must first localize so the shifted stopped process is nonnegative.

front: Doob decomposition (4.3 Examples)
back: Every submartingale decomposes uniquely into a martingale plus an increasing predictable process starting at zero. Exam use: Separates random fluctuation from predictable drift in counting-process and indicator-sum arguments. Pitfall: The compensator is predictable, not optional; its increment is known one step earlier.

front: Conditional Borel-Cantelli lemma (4.3 Examples)
back: For adapted events B_n, occurrence infinitely often is controlled by the divergence or convergence of the conditional probability sums E(1_{B_n}|F_{n-1}). Exam use: Use for dependent trials where ordinary independence is unavailable but conditional probabilities are tractable. Pitfall: The conditioning must be on the past; replacing it by unconditional probabilities loses the theorem's content.

front: Polya urn proportion martingale (4.3.2 Polya's Urn Scheme)
back: In Polya's urn, the fraction of balls of one color is a bounded martingale and therefore converges almost surely. Exam use: A model example for bounded martingale convergence and beta-limit intuition. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence alone does not identify the limiting distribution without extra computation.

front: Radon-Nikodym derivative martingales (4.3.3 Radon-Nikodym Derivatives)
back: If mu_n and nu_n are restrictions to F_n with mu_n absolutely continuous with respect to nu_n, then dmu_n/dnu_n forms a nonnegative martingale under nu. Exam use: Use to compare infinite product measures and prove absolute-continuity versus singularity alternatives. Pitfall: Finite-dimensional absolute continuity does not by itself guarantee infinite-dimensional absolute continuity.

front: Branching process normalized martingale (4.3.4 Branching Processes)
back: For a Galton-Watson process with mean mu, Z_n/mu^n is a nonnegative martingale when mu is positive. Exam use: The central martingale for extinction, survival, and supercritical growth questions. Pitfall: In the supercritical case the martingale limit may still be zero unless an L log L condition holds.

front: Bounded optional sampling inequality (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: If X is a submartingale and N is a bounded stopping time, then E X_0 <= E X_N <= E X_k for a bound k on N. Exam use: The bounded-time version of optional sampling used inside maximal inequality proofs. Pitfall: Boundedness of the stopping time is doing real work; unbounded stopping can fail.

front: Doob maximal inequality (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: For a submartingale, lambda P(max_{m<=n} X_m >= lambda) is bounded by the expected terminal value on the event that the maximum exceeds lambda. Exam use: Use to control suprema of martingales, derive Kolmogorov inequalities, and prove Lp convergence. Pitfall: Apply it to nonnegative submartingales such as |X_n| or X_n^2 when signs are an issue.

front: Doob Lp maximum inequality (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: For p>1, the Lp norm of max_{m<=n} |X_m| is bounded by p/(p-1) times the Lp norm of X_n for martingales and suitable submartingales. Exam use: The main route from uniform Lp bounds to almost sure and Lp convergence. Pitfall: There is no comparable L1 maximal inequality without extra hypotheses.

front: Lp martingale convergence theorem (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: If a martingale has sup_n E|X_n|^p finite for some p>1, then X_n converges almost surely and in Lp. Exam use: A clean sufficient condition for exchanging martingale limits with p-th moments. Pitfall: The theorem needs p>1; L1 convergence requires uniform integrability instead.

front: Orthogonality of square-integrable martingale increments (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: Square-integrable martingale increments are orthogonal, so variances of sums add across increments. Exam use: Use for L2 bounds, variance computations, and quadratic martingale arguments. Pitfall: Orthogonality is not independence; it is a second-moment identity.

front: Conditional variance formula for martingales (4.4 Doob's Inequality, Convergence in Lp, p>1)
back: For square-integrable martingales, conditional second-moment growth is the sum of conditional variances of increments. Exam use: Use to estimate quadratic variation and prove convergence from summable conditional variances. Pitfall: Use conditional variances of increments, not unconditional variances unless independence or identical structure justifies it.

front: Square-integrable martingales and quadratic variation (4.5 Square Integrable Martingales*)
back: For square-integrable martingales, the predictable accumulated conditional variance A_n governs maximal L2 bounds and convergence. Exam use: Use when convergence depends on summability of conditional variances rather than bounded increments. Pitfall: The predictable quadratic variation is not the same as the realized squared-increment sum.

front: Martingale strong-law normalization (4.5 Square Integrable Martingales*)
back: If a square-integrable martingale has controlled predictable quadratic variation, then X_n normalized by a suitable increasing function of A_n tends to zero. Exam use: Generalizes Kolmogorov strong-law arguments to martingale differences. Pitfall: Choose a normalization satisfying the required integral/summability condition.

front: Uniform integrability (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: A family is uniformly integrable when the tail expectations sup E(|X|; |X|>K) go to zero as K goes to infinity. Exam use: The exact condition needed to upgrade probability or a.s. convergence to L1 convergence. Pitfall: Bounded L1 norms alone do not imply uniform integrability.

front: Uniform integrability plus probability convergence gives L1 convergence (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: If X_n is uniformly integrable and X_n converges in probability to X, then X is integrable and X_n converges to X in L1. Exam use: Use after proving almost sure martingale convergence to obtain L1 convergence. Pitfall: Convergence in probability without uniform integrability is not enough for L1 convergence.

front: Submartingale L1 convergence equivalences (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: For submartingales, uniform integrability is equivalent to almost sure and L1 convergence to an integrable limit under the standard convergence hypotheses. Exam use: Decides when the limit preserves first moments. Pitfall: Almost sure convergence from Chapter 4.2 by itself is weaker than L1 convergence.

front: Closed martingales and uniform integrability (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: A martingale is uniformly integrable iff it converges in L1 iff it can be written as X_n=E(X|F_n) for some integrable terminal variable X. Exam use: The main structural test for optional stopping at unbounded times. Pitfall: A martingale can converge a.s. but fail to be closed by an L1 terminal variable.

front: Levy upward theorem (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: If F_n increases to F_infinity, then E(X|F_n) converges almost surely and in L1 to E(X|F_infinity). Exam use: Use to prove Levy's 0-1 law and conditional dominated convergence. Pitfall: The limiting sigma-field is generated by the increasing union, not simply the pointwise union as a collection.

front: Levy's 0-1 law (4.6 Uniform Integrability, Convergence in L1)
back: If A belongs to the limiting sigma-field of an increasing filtration, then P(A|F_n) converges almost surely to 1_A. Exam use: Use to convert better and better finite information into eventual certainty for tail-like events inside the generated limit. Pitfall: This is different from Kolmogorov's 0-1 law, which concerns independent tail sigma-fields.

front: Backward martingale convergence (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: A martingale indexed backward in time, with decreasing sigma-fields, converges almost surely and in L1 to its conditional expectation on the tail intersection. Exam use: The engine behind the strong law, Hewitt-Savage, and de Finetti-style results. Pitfall: The index direction matters: sigma-fields decrease as time moves backward.

front: Levy downward theorem (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: If F_n decreases to F_{-infinity}, then E(Y|F_n) converges almost surely and in L1 to E(Y|F_{-infinity}). Exam use: Use for exchangeability, tail sigma-fields, and reverse filtrations. Pitfall: Do not replace the intersection by a limiting union; this is the downward theorem.

front: Strong law via backward martingales (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: For iid integrable variables, sample averages can be represented through decreasing symmetric-information sigma-fields and converge to the mean. Exam use: A conceptual proof of the strong law that also prepares de Finetti arguments. Pitfall: The iid and integrability assumptions are doing the work; this is not a variance proof.

front: Hewitt-Savage 0-1 law (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: For iid sequences, every event invariant under finite permutations has probability 0 or 1. Exam use: Use when an event is exchangeable rather than merely tail-measurable. Pitfall: Permutation invariance is stronger/different than tail measurability; check the event carefully.

front: de Finetti theorem for exchangeable sequences (4.7 Backwards Martingales)
back: Exchangeable sequences are conditionally iid given the exchangeable tail information; in the binary case they are mixtures of iid Bernoulli sequences. Exam use: Recognize hidden-random-parameter representations in exchangeable models. Pitfall: Exchangeability is weaker than independence but stronger than identical marginal distributions.

front: Optional stopping for uniformly integrable martingales (4.8 Optional Stopping Theorems)
back: Uniformly integrable martingales satisfy optional sampling for arbitrary stopping times, with stopped values inheriting the martingale inequalities. Exam use: The cleanest condition under which E X_N = E X_0 is valid for unbounded N. Pitfall: Without uniform integrability or another sufficient condition, optional stopping can fail dramatically.

front: Optional stopping with integrable terminal value (4.8 Optional Stopping Theorems)
back: If X_N is integrable and the pre-stopping tails X_n 1_{N>n} are uniformly integrable, then X_{N wedge n} is uniformly integrable and optional stopping applies. Exam use: A practical checklist for hitting times where the stopped value is bounded or controlled. Pitfall: Finite expectation of N alone is not the same as this UI tail condition, though bounded increments can imply useful variants.

front: Wald's equation via optional stopping (4.8 Optional Stopping Theorems)
back: For iid integrable increments with mean mu, S_N has expectation mu E N under suitable stopping and integrability conditions. Exam use: Use for expected sums at stopping times, especially random walks and renewal-style counts. Pitfall: Independence of N from increments is not the right general condition; N is often a stopping time, and optional stopping hypotheses must be checked.

front: Symmetric gambler's ruin via martingales (4.8.1 Applications to Random Walks)
back: For symmetric simple random walk stopped on hitting a or b, optional stopping of S_n gives linear hitting probabilities and S_n^2-n gives expected hitting times. Exam use: High-yield prelim template for hitting probabilities and mean exit times. Pitfall: For one-sided unbounded hitting times, use truncation or extra arguments before sending the bound to infinity.

front: Asymmetric gambler's ruin via exponential martingales (4.8.1 Applications to Random Walks)
back: For biased simple random walk, an exponential martingale r^{S_n} gives nonlinear hitting probabilities and finite expected hitting times in the drift direction. Exam use: Use when the walk has drift and the harmonic function is exponential rather than linear. Pitfall: The symmetric formulas are limiting cases; plugging p=q into biased formulas may require taking a limit.

front: Insurance ruin exponential bound (4.8.1 Applications to Random Walks)
back: If a random walk has a positive adjustment coefficient theta with E exp(theta xi)=1, exponential martingales bound the probability of ever hitting a bad lower level. Exam use: Use for Cramer-Lundberg style ruin bounds and rare-event hitting estimates. Pitfall: The bound needs the exponential moment equation at a positive theta.

front: Reflection principle (4.9 Combinatorics of Simple Random Walk*)
back: For simple random walk paths, paths crossing a barrier can be counted by reflecting the path after the first hit. Exam use: Use for exact hitting and maximum distributions without martingale machinery. Pitfall: The reflection is a path-counting bijection; keep parity and endpoints consistent.

front: Ballot theorem (4.9 Combinatorics of Simple Random Walk*)
back: If one candidate finishes ahead, the probability that they stayed strictly ahead throughout has a simple ratio depending on the final margin. Exam use: Use for positivity-conditioned walks and first-passage combinatorics. Pitfall: Strict positivity versus nonnegativity changes the boundary and constants.

front: Arcsine laws for simple random walk (4.9 Combinatorics of Simple Random Walk*)
back: The last zero time and the occupation time above zero for long symmetric simple random walks have arcsine limiting distributions. Exam use: Recognize that typical paths spend a surprisingly large fraction of time mostly positive or mostly negative. Pitfall: The arcsine density is U-shaped; intuition based on concentration near one half is wrong.

front: Countable-state Markov property (5.1 Examples)
back: A temporally homogeneous countable-state Markov chain satisfies P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n=i, past)=P(X_{n+1}=j | X_n=i)=p(i,j). Exam use: Use this as the first diagnostic for deciding whether a proposed process is Markov and for computing transition probabilities. Pitfall: A function of a Markov chain need not be Markov unless the retained state contains enough information.

front: Random walk as a Markov chain (5.1 Examples)
back: If X_n=X_0+xi_1+...+xi_n with iid increments on Z^d, then p(i,j)=mu({j-i}). Exam use: Use whenever increments are independent and stationary; transition probabilities depend only on displacement. Pitfall: A random walk often has no finite equilibrium on infinite groups even though it is Markov.

front: Branching process transition kernel (5.1 Examples)
back: For a Galton-Watson process, p(i,j)=P(xi_1+...+xi_i=j), where each current individual independently produces offspring. Exam use: Recognize absorbing extinction state 0 and use generating functions for transition calculations. Pitfall: The process is Markov in the population size, but it typically does not converge to a nontrivial equilibrium.

front: Ehrenfest urn chain (5.1 Examples)
back: With r balls and state k equal to the number in the first urn, p(k,k+1)=(r-k)/r and p(k,k-1)=k/r. Exam use: A canonical finite birth-death chain for reversibility, stationary distributions, and periodicity. Pitfall: The chain alternates parity, so convergence of p^n(k,j) must account for period 2 unless laziness is added.

front: Wright-Fisher transition law (5.1 Examples)
back: In a population of N genes, the number of A alleles has binomial transition p(i,j)=C(N,j) rho_i^j(1-rho_i)^{N-j}; without mutation rho_i=i/N, with mutation rho_i=(i/N)(1-u)+((N-i)/N)v. Exam use: Use for absorption/fixation problems, martingale hitting probabilities, and finite-chain equilibrium with mutation. Pitfall: Without mutation, 0 and N are absorbing; with positive mutation rates they are not.

front: M/G/1 embedded queue chain (5.1 Examples)
back: At service-start epochs, X_{n+1}=(X_n+xi_{n+1})^+, where P(xi=k-1)=a_k and a_k is the chance k arrivals occur during one service time. Exam use: Use drift E xi=mean arrivals per service minus 1 to classify recurrence and stability. Pitfall: Do not confuse the continuous-time queue with the embedded discrete chain at service-start times.

front: General transition probability (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: On a measurable state space, p(x,A) is a transition probability when A -> p(x,A) is a probability measure and x -> p(x,A) is measurable. Exam use: Use this language for chains beyond countable state spaces, including queues and continuous densities. Pitfall: For general spaces, p(x,y) is usually not a number; the kernel is p(x,A).

front: Finite-dimensional construction of a chain (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: Given initial law mu and kernel p, the finite-dimensional laws are iterated integrals mu(dx_0)p(x_0,dx_1)...p(x_{n-1},dx_n). Exam use: Justifies statements that a Markov chain with a specified initial distribution and transition kernel exists. Pitfall: The formula gives path probabilities only for measurable cylinder events, then extension supplies the full path law.

front: Transition operator notation (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: For bounded measurable f, Pf(x)=E_x f(X_1)=integral f(y)p(x,dy); iterates P^n describe n-step expectations. Exam use: Useful for harmonic equations, hitting probabilities, expected hitting times, and martingale checks. Pitfall: The same symbol p may denote a kernel or a matrix; P as an operator acts on functions.

front: Markov property with path shifts (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: For bounded future functionals Y, E_x[Y o theta_n | F_n]=E_{X_n}Y. Exam use: Transforms future-path questions after time n into a fresh chain started at X_n. Pitfall: The future functional must be evaluated on the shifted path; forgetting the shift changes the event.

front: Chapman-Kolmogorov equations (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: The n-step kernels satisfy p^{m+n}(x,z)=sum_y p^m(x,y)p^n(y,z) in countable spaces, with the analogous integral formula in general spaces. Exam use: Compute multi-step transitions, prove communication, and derive renewal decompositions for return probabilities. Pitfall: For general spaces, replace sums over y by integration against p^m(x,dy).

front: Strong Markov property (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: At a stopping time T, the post-T process behaves like a fresh chain started from X_T on the event T<infinity. Exam use: The main engine for hitting-time decompositions, renewal arguments, recurrence proofs, and reflection arguments. Pitfall: T must be a stopping time; optional-looking random times that see the future do not qualify.

front: Hitting and return time decomposition (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: Events involving visits to a state or set can be decomposed at the first hitting time and restarted by the strong Markov property. Exam use: Use for equations like P_x(T_z<infinity) >= P_x(T_y<infinity)P_y(T_z<infinity) and for first-step analysis. Pitfall: Use T_y=inf{n>=1:X_n=y} for returns and V_A=inf{n>=0:X_n in A} for entrance; the indexing matters.

front: Reflection principle for symmetric random walk (5.2 Construction, Markov Properties)
back: For one-dimensional simple symmetric random walk, paths crossing a level can be reflected after the first hitting time to convert maximum events into endpoint events. Exam use: Fast route to distributions of maxima and hitting probabilities for simple random walk. Pitfall: The clean reflection bijection uses symmetry and nearest-neighbor increments; it does not transfer unchanged to arbitrary walks.

front: Expected visits criterion (5.3 Recurrence and Transience)
back: A state y is recurrent iff E_y N(y)=sum_n p^n(y,y)=infinity; if transient this expected visit count is finite. Exam use: Use Green-function sums to classify states, especially for random walks. Pitfall: The criterion is for visits starting from y; from another x, accessibility must be handled separately.

front: Recurrence transfers through communication (5.3 Recurrence and Transience)
back: If x is recurrent and rho_xy>0, then y is recurrent and rho_yx=1. Exam use: Shows recurrence is a class property inside communicating classes. Pitfall: Accessibility in only one direction is not symmetric unless recurrence is known.

front: Finite closed classes contain recurrent states (5.3 Recurrence and Transience)
back: Every finite closed set contains a recurrent state; if the finite closed set is irreducible, all its states are recurrent. Exam use: Use to classify finite Markov chains and prove absorbing/recurrent structure. Pitfall: The set must be closed; otherwise the chain can leave and never return.

front: Decomposition theorem (5.3 Recurrence and Transience)
back: The recurrent states split into disjoint irreducible closed classes, while every transient state is visited only finitely often almost surely. Exam use: Organizes the long-run behavior of countable chains before stationary distributions enter. Pitfall: Closed recurrent classes need not be finite; irreducibility is within each class, not necessarily the whole state space.

front: Foster-type recurrence criterion (5.3 Recurrence and Transience)
back: For an irreducible chain, a nonnegative function phi with E_x phi(X_1)<=phi(x) off a finite set and phi(x)->infinity can force recurrence. Exam use: A powerful recurrence test for birth-death chains, queues, and chains with inward drift. Pitfall: Check the direction of drift carefully; superharmonic behavior outside a finite set is the recurrence signal.

front: Birth-death scale function (5.3 Recurrence and Transience)
back: For a birth-death chain, harmonic scale functions solve p_x h(x+1)+q_x h(x-1)=h(x) and give explicit two-sided hitting probabilities. Exam use: Use for gambler's ruin probabilities and recurrence tests on {0,1,2,...}. Pitfall: Boundary conventions at 0 matter; write the model's p_0 and q_0 before applying formulas.

front: Birth-death recurrence test (5.3 Recurrence and Transience)
back: For a birth-death chain on nonnegative integers, recurrence of 0 is determined by divergence of the scale sum built from products q_i/p_i. Exam use: High-yield test for nearest-neighbor chains with state-dependent drift. Pitfall: Small asymptotic drift terms such as C/j can decide the boundary between recurrence and transience.

front: Recurrent values form a closed subgroup (5.4 Recurrence of Random Walks Starred Section)
back: For a random walk on R^d, the set of recurrent values is either empty or a closed subgroup of R^d. Exam use: Clarifies what recurrence can mean outside lattice state spaces. Pitfall: Returning near a value is a topological notion in R^d, not necessarily exact equality.

front: Equivalent random-walk recurrence criteria (5.4 Recurrence of Random Walks Starred Section)
back: For random walks, recurrence can be characterized by infinitely many visits to neighborhoods, divergence of occupation sums, and Green-function criteria. Exam use: Choose the criterion that matches the data: local probabilities, characteristic functions, or limit theorems. Pitfall: The exact form differs between lattice and nonlattice settings; keep the state-space topology visible.

front: Simple random walk recurrence by dimension (5.4 Recurrence of Random Walks Starred Section)
back: Simple random walk is recurrent in dimensions 1 and 2 and transient in dimensions 3 and higher. Exam use: A central classification result and a common comparison model for Markov-chain recurrence. Pitfall: Parity affects exact return times but not the convergence/divergence classification.

front: Chung-Fuchs one-dimensional recurrence pattern (5.4 Recurrence of Random Walks Starred Section)
back: In one dimension, centered weak-law behavior is the key condition behind recurrence for broad random walks. Exam use: Use when increments are not nearest-neighbor but have no drift on the natural scale. Pitfall: Nonzero drift typically pushes the walk to infinity and destroys recurrence.

front: Two-dimensional normal-limit recurrence (5.4 Recurrence of Random Walks Starred Section)
back: A two-dimensional random walk with S_n/sqrt(n) converging to a nondegenerate normal law is recurrent. Exam use: Recognize recurrence for centered finite-variance planar walks. Pitfall: A degenerate limiting covariance needs separate lower-dimensional analysis.

front: Fourier recurrence criterion (5.4 Recurrence of Random Walks Starred Section)
back: Random-walk recurrence can be tested through integrals involving 1/(1-phi(t)), where phi is the increment characteristic function. Exam use: Useful for non-nearest-neighbor walks and for proving no truly three-dimensional walk is recurrent. Pitfall: The singularity near t=0 is decisive; away from zero the integral is usually harmless.

front: No truly three-dimensional recurrent random walk (5.4 Recurrence of Random Walks Starred Section)
back: A random walk with genuinely three-dimensional increment support cannot be recurrent. Exam use: Use as a broad transience result beyond simple random walk. Pitfall: If the walk actually lives in a lower-dimensional subgroup, reduce the dimension before applying the theorem.

front: Stationary measures and distributions (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: A measure mu is stationary when mu p=mu; if mu(S)=1 it is a stationary distribution. Exam use: Find equilibria, normalize invariant measures, and connect long-run proportions to return times. Pitfall: An infinite stationary measure need not be a probability distribution and does not by itself imply equilibrium probabilities.

front: Reversibility and detailed balance (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: If mu(x)p(x,y)=mu(y)p(y,x) for all x,y, then mu is stationary and the chain is reversible under mu. Exam use: The fastest way to find stationary distributions for birth-death, Ehrenfest, and many urn chains. Pitfall: Detailed balance is sufficient but not necessary for stationarity unless reversibility is required.

front: Kolmogorov cycle condition (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: For an irreducible chain, reversibility is characterized by equality of transition-probability products around every directed cycle and its reverse. Exam use: Use to test whether a proposed chain can be reversible without solving for pi first. Pitfall: The condition is about all cycles; checking only two-state moves can miss circulation.

front: Stationary measure from one recurrent cycle (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: If x is recurrent and T_x is the first positive return time, then expected occupation counts before T_x define a stationary measure with mass 1 at x. Exam use: Construct invariant measures for recurrent irreducible chains without guessing. Pitfall: The measure may have infinite total mass when the chain is null recurrent.

front: Uniqueness of stationary measure in irreducible recurrent chains (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: For an irreducible recurrent countable chain, the stationary measure is unique up to constant multiples. Exam use: Lets you identify all invariant measures once one candidate is found. Pitfall: Uniqueness up to scale does not mean a stationary distribution exists; total mass may be infinite.

front: Stationary probability and mean return time (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: For an irreducible chain with stationary distribution pi, pi(y)=1/E_y T_y, so states with finite mean return time are positive recurrent. Exam use: Compute stationary probabilities through expected return times and classify positive versus null recurrence. Pitfall: Recurrence only says T_y is finite almost surely; positive recurrence requires finite expectation.

front: Positive recurrence equivalences (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: For an irreducible chain, existence of a stationary distribution, finite mean return times, and positive recurrence are equivalent. Exam use: A standard checklist for proving an irreducible chain has an equilibrium law. Pitfall: In infinite state spaces, irreducible recurrent chains can be null recurrent and fail to have a stationary distribution.

front: Birth-death stationary distribution (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: For birth-death chains, detailed balance gives pi(x) proportional to products of birth rates divided by death rates, if the normalizing sum is finite. Exam use: Fast equilibrium computation for queues, Ehrenfest-type chains, and one-dimensional population models. Pitfall: The unnormalized weights may define an invariant measure even when their sum diverges.

front: M/G/1 stability and stationarity (5.5 Stationary Measures)
back: For the embedded M/G/1 queue, stability/positive recurrence occurs when the mean number of arrivals during one service time is less than 1. Exam use: Use drift less than zero as the queue stability condition. Pitfall: Recurrence and stationary behavior fail at or above critical load in different ways; do not treat mu=1 as stable.

front: Renewal limit for return probabilities (5.6 Asymptotic Behavior)
back: If y is recurrent, then p^n(y,y) has asymptotics governed by the renewal theorem for the return-time distribution; positive recurrence gives limits tied to 1/E_y T_y. Exam use: Explains why return-time periods and mean cycle lengths control long-run transition probabilities. Pitfall: Periodicity can force p^n(y,y)=0 on many n, so limits may only exist along arithmetic subsequences.

front: Period of a state (5.6 Asymptotic Behavior)
back: The period of state x is the greatest common divisor of {n>=1:p^n(x,x)>0}; irreducible communicating states share the same period. Exam use: Check whether convergence to stationarity can hold at every time or only along residue classes. Pitfall: A chain can be irreducible and positive recurrent but still fail ordinary convergence because of period greater than 1.

front: Aperiodic convergence theorem (5.6 Asymptotic Behavior)
back: If a countable chain is irreducible, aperiodic, and has stationary distribution pi, then p^n(x,y) -> pi(y). Exam use: Main equilibrium convergence theorem for prelim Markov-chain problems. Pitfall: Irreducible and stationary are not enough; without aperiodicity the raw n-step probabilities may oscillate.

front: Coupling bound for convergence (5.6 Asymptotic Behavior)
back: If two copies of a Markov chain are run together after meeting, then total variation distance is bounded by the probability their coupling time exceeds n. Exam use: Use for finite irreducible aperiodic chains and for quantitative convergence estimates. Pitfall: The coupling must preserve the correct marginal transition law for each coordinate.

front: Strong law for additive functionals (5.6 Asymptotic Behavior)
back: For an irreducible positive recurrent chain with stationary law pi, time averages of suitable f(X_n) converge to sum_x pi(x)f(x). Exam use: Use to turn long-run sample averages into stationary expectations. Pitfall: Integrability under pi is essential; positive recurrence alone does not control arbitrary unbounded rewards.

front: Periodic cyclic classes (5.7 Periodicity, Tail Sigma-Field Starred Section)
back: For an irreducible recurrent chain of period d, the state space splits into d cyclic classes and the d-step chain on each class is aperiodic. Exam use: Use to repair convergence statements for periodic chains by passing to subsequences n congruent to r mod d. Pitfall: The stationary distribution is still a distribution on all states, but mass is visited cyclically rather than approached at all times.

front: Periodic convergence theorem (5.7 Periodicity, Tail Sigma-Field Starred Section)
back: For irreducible positive recurrent chains with period d, p^n(x,y) converges along the compatible residue class to d*pi(y) and is zero on incompatible residues. Exam use: Use when examples such as simple random walk on bipartite graphs oscillate forever. Pitfall: The factor d appears because the d-step chain's stationary law is pi restricted to one cyclic class and renormalized.

front: Tail sigma-field triviality via coupling (5.7 Periodicity, Tail Sigma-Field Starred Section)
back: For suitable irreducible recurrent chains, tail events have probability 0 or 1 because coupled copies eventually share the same future behavior. Exam use: Use for zero-one laws about eventual behavior of recurrent Markov chains and simple random walk. Pitfall: Tail events are not the same as invariant events at a fixed time; they ignore every finite prefix.

front: Harris recurrence (5.8 General State Space Starred Section)
back: A general-state Markov chain is Harris recurrent when it repeatedly hits every set of positive reference measure from relevant starting points. Exam use: Use as the general-state analogue of irreducible recurrence. Pitfall: In continuous spaces, single points often have probability zero, so point-return definitions are too strong or meaningless.

front: Small sets and split-chain idea (5.8 General State Space Starred Section)
back: A small set has a minorization that lets one split transitions into regeneration attempts and residual motion. Exam use: The conceptual bridge from countable recurrent-chain proofs to Harris-chain stationary and convergence theorems. Pitfall: Minorization is a structural condition; it is not just positive probability of hitting a set.

front: Stationary measure for recurrent Harris chains (5.8 General State Space Starred Section)
back: In the recurrent Harris setting, there is a sigma-finite stationary measure, and it is unique up to scale under the appropriate irreducibility assumptions. Exam use: Generalizes countable-state invariant-measure construction to continuous state spaces. Pitfall: Sigma-finite stationary measure is not automatically a stationary distribution; total mass decides positive recurrence.

front: Aperiodic Harris convergence theorem (5.8 General State Space Starred Section)
back: An aperiodic positive recurrent Harris chain with stationary distribution converges to that stationary distribution in total variation from suitable starting laws. Exam use: Use for continuous-state Markov models where transition densities make pointwise matrix methods unavailable. Pitfall: The theorem requires Harris recurrence and aperiodicity; a stationary density alone may not give convergence from every start.

front: GI/G/1 queue as a reflected random walk (5.8 General State Space Starred Section)
back: The waiting-time recursion W_{n+1}=(W_n+eta_n-zeta_n)^+ is a general-state Markov chain and storage model. Exam use: Use drift E eta < E zeta for stability and Harris-chain tools for stationary waiting-time distributions. Pitfall: The embedded workload is continuous-state, so countable transition-matrix formulas do not apply directly.

front: Stationary sequence (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: A sequence X_0, X_1, ... is stationary when each shifted finite block has the same law as the original block. Exam use: Use as the entry point for ergodic averages, stationary Markov chains, rotations, and stationary increments. Pitfall: Stationarity is distributional, not pathwise equality; it does not imply independence.

front: Measure-preserving transformation model (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: Every stationary sequence on a nice state space can be represented as X_n(omega)=X(phi^n omega) for a measure-preserving map phi. Exam use: Lets all ergodic-theorem statements be proved for one abstract dynamical system. Pitfall: The representation changes the sample space; it preserves laws but not necessarily the user's original probability space.

front: Invariant events and invariant sigma-field (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: An event A is invariant if phi^{-1}A=A up to null sets; the invariant events form the sigma-field I. Exam use: The limit in Birkhoff's theorem is the conditional expectation onto I. Pitfall: Invariant means unchanged under the transformation, not merely high probability overlap after one shift.

front: Ergodicity (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: A measure-preserving transformation is ergodic when every invariant event has probability 0 or 1. Exam use: Use ergodicity to turn Birkhoff limits E(X|I) into constants EX. Pitfall: Ergodic is stronger than stationary and different from independent; periodic examples can be stationary but not mixing.

front: IID shifts are ergodic (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: For an iid sequence, invariant shift events lie in the tail sigma-field, so Kolmogorov's 0-1 law implies ergodicity. Exam use: Explains why Birkhoff's theorem recovers the strong law for iid sequences. Pitfall: The tail sigma-field argument uses iid independence; it is not automatic for every stationary sequence.

front: Stationary Markov chain ergodicity criterion (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: A countable-state Markov chain started in a positive stationary distribution is ergodic exactly when the chain is irreducible. Exam use: Use for time averages of irreducible positive recurrent chains. Pitfall: Irreducibility makes I trivial, but periodic chains may still have a nontrivial tail sigma-field.

front: Circle rotation ergodicity (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: Rotation x -> x+theta mod 1 is ergodic under Lebesgue measure when theta is irrational and not ergodic when theta is rational. Exam use: The canonical deterministic example where ergodic averages behave like probabilistic averages. Pitfall: Rational rotations have finite orbits and many invariant unions of orbit slices.

front: Functions of stationary processes (6.1 Definitions and Examples)
back: Measurable functions of the future of a stationary process form another stationary process; ergodicity is inherited under such factors. Exam use: Useful for creating stationary indicator, block, or return-time processes. Pitfall: The function must be applied in a shift-compatible way; arbitrary time-dependent functions can destroy stationarity.

front: Birkhoff ergodic theorem (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For X in L1, the time averages n^{-1} sum_{m<n} X(phi^m omega) converge almost surely and in L1 to E(X|I). Exam use: Main tool for replacing long-run empirical averages by conditional or ordinary means. Pitfall: Without ergodicity the limit is random: it is E(X|I), not necessarily EX.

front: Maximal ergodic lemma (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For partial sums S_k of X along a measure-preserving orbit and M_k=max(0,S_1,...,S_k), one has E(X; M_k>0) >= 0. Exam use: The key technical inequality behind Birkhoff's pointwise convergence proof. Pitfall: It is an integration inequality, not a direct maximal probability bound; track the set {M_k>0}.

front: Strong law as an ergodic theorem (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For iid X_m in L1, Birkhoff gives n^{-1} sum_{m<n} X_m -> E X_0 almost surely and in L1. Exam use: Use as a conceptual shortcut from Chapter 2 LLNs to general stationary sequences. Pitfall: The theorem needs integrability; for nonintegrable iid variables this conclusion can fail.

front: Stationary Markov chain time averages (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For an irreducible countable-state Markov chain started from its stationary distribution pi, n^{-1} sum_{m<n} f(X_m) -> sum_x f(x) pi(x) for pi-integrable f. Exam use: Recovers the long-run frequency and reward law for positive recurrent Markov chains. Pitfall: The statement is under stationarity; nonstationary initial laws need an additional argument.

front: Weyl equidistribution from irrational rotation (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: For irrational theta, the orbit x+n theta mod 1 visits every interval [a,b) with limiting frequency b-a for every starting x. Exam use: Use for deterministic averaging and number-theoretic distribution mod 1. Pitfall: Birkhoff first gives almost-every starting point; the interval theorem needs extra work for every x.

front: Benford law for powers (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: If log_10 2 is irrational, the first digit of 2^m has limiting frequency log_10((k+1)/k) for digit k. Exam use: A memorable application of equidistribution to leading-digit asymptotics. Pitfall: Benford frequencies describe a limiting distribution, not exact finite-sample frequencies.

front: Lp convergence upgrade (6.2 Birkhoff's Ergodic Theorem)
back: If X is in Lp for p>1, the Birkhoff averages converge in Lp as well as almost surely. Exam use: Useful when a problem asks for convergence in norm rather than only almost surely. Pitfall: Do not infer Lp convergence from L1 convergence without an Lp bound or uniform integrability.

front: Range growth for stationary increments (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For a stationary-increment walk S_n, the range R_n=|{S_1,...,S_n}| satisfies R_n/n -> E(1_A|I), where A is the event of never returning to 0 after time 0. Exam use: Connects recurrence questions to an ergodic average of escape events. Pitfall: The limit is conditional on I; only in ergodic cases does it reduce to P(A).

front: Zero conditional drift implies recurrence on Z (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For stationary integer-valued increments with E|X_1|<infinity and E(X_1|I)=0, the walk returns to 0 infinitely often almost surely. Exam use: Generalizes the Chung-Fuchs recurrence intuition beyond iid increments. Pitfall: The condition is E(X_1|I)=0, not merely EX_1=0; mixtures of positive and negative drifts are counterexamples.

front: Kac return-time theorem (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For a stationary process that hits A almost surely, the successive return gaps to A are stationary under P(.|X_0 in A), and E(T_1|X_0 in A)=1/P(X_0 in A). Exam use: Turns visits to a set into regenerative cycles and recovers mean return time 1/pi(x) for Markov chains. Pitfall: Conditioning on X_0 in A is essential; the first waiting time from an arbitrary stationary time has a length-biased flavor.

front: Cycle trick for stationary measures (6.3 Recurrence)
back: Expected occupation of B during a return cycle to A equals P(X_0 in B)/P(X_0 in A) when A and B are disjoint and A is recurrent. Exam use: Useful for constructing stationary measures of Markov chains from excursions. Pitfall: The denominator is the stationary mass of A; the formula is not a conditional probability of hitting B before returning.

front: Stationary renewal waiting-time bias (6.3 Recurrence)
back: For a stationary {0,1} process conditioned on X_0=1, the first return law satisfies Pbar(T_1=n)=Pbar(T_1>=n)/Ebar T_1 in the iid-gap renewal case. Exam use: Explains inspection-paradox corrections in stationary renewal processes. Pitfall: The distribution seen from a stationary time differs from the raw interarrival distribution.

front: Subadditive ergodic theorem (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: For a stationary subadditive array X_{m,n} with integrability and a linear lower expectation bound, X_{0,n}/n converges almost surely and in L1 to a limit with mean inf_m E X_{0,m}/m. Exam use: The main tool for limits of optimal costs, passage times, longest subsequences, and products. Pitfall: Check the exact stationarity assumptions; Liggett's version is weaker than Kingman's original in useful applications.

front: Fekete step for expected subadditive arrays (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: If a_n=E X_{0,n} and a_m+a_{n-m}>=a_n, then a_n/n converges to inf_m a_m/m. Exam use: Use whenever the deterministic expectation part of a subadditive theorem appears. Pitfall: The inequality direction is easy to mix up because the array convention is X_{0,m}+X_{m,n}>=X_{0,n}.

front: Birkhoff as the additive special case (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: If X_{m,n}=xi_{m+1}+...+xi_n for a stationary integrable sequence, then the subadditive theorem reduces to Birkhoff. Exam use: A quick consistency check for the hypotheses of the subadditive theorem. Pitfall: Do not lose the indexing: X_{m,n} covers increments after m through n.

front: Range as a subadditive example (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: The number of distinct sites visited between times m+1 and n is subadditive, so the normalized range has an almost sure and L1 limit. Exam use: Provides a second route to existence of range-growth rates even when the rate is not identified. Pitfall: The theorem gives existence of the limit, not the recurrence classification by itself.

front: Longest common subsequence limit (6.4 A Subadditive Ergodic Theorem)
back: For two ergodic stationary sequences, the longest common subsequence length L_{0,n} satisfies L_{0,n}/n -> gamma, where gamma=sup_m E L_{0,m}/m. Exam use: Recognize optimization lengths as candidates for subadditive limits. Pitfall: The theorem proves existence of gamma but usually not its numerical value.

front: Products of positive random matrices (6.5 Applications)
back: For stationary positive matrices with integrable log entries, n^{-1} log of product entries converges almost surely to a common growth rate. Exam use: A probability route to Lyapunov-exponent-type limits. Pitfall: Strict positivity and log-integrability matter; zeros or heavy tails can break the logarithmic comparison.

front: Increasing subsequences in random permutations (6.5 Applications)
back: Poissonizing random permutations and applying subadditivity shows the longest increasing subsequence has an n^{1/2} order limit constant. Exam use: A classic example of turning a combinatorial optimization problem into an ergodic limit. Pitfall: The subadditive argument gives the existence of the constant; identifying it as 2 requires deeper work.

front: Poisson square time change (6.5 Applications)
back: If tau(n) is the side length of the square containing the first n points of a rate-one planar Poisson process, then tau(n)/sqrt(n) -> 1 almost surely. Exam use: Used to pass from Poissonized increasing paths back to fixed-size random permutations. Pitfall: The scale is sqrt(n) because the square area is t^2.

front: First-passage percolation time constant (6.5 Applications)
back: For iid nonnegative edge passage times on Z^d with suitable integrability, t(0,nu)/n converges almost surely to a deterministic time constant. Exam use: Core template for metric growth models and random media. Pitfall: Finite mean of one edge is sufficient in the simple statement but can be weakened; without integrability the limit may be infinite.

front: First-passage percolation integrability condition (6.5 Applications)
back: A weaker useful condition is that the minimum of 2d independent edge times has finite mean, equivalently integral_0^infty (1-F(x))^{2d} dx < infinity. Exam use: Helps check when a finite time constant exists for heavy-tailed edge weights. Pitfall: This condition concerns the minimum of several edges, not the edge variable itself.

front: Age-dependent branching process speed (6.5 Applications)
back: In an age-dependent branching process with no extinction at birth and finite lifetimes, the first birth time in generation n divided by n converges almost surely. Exam use: Shows why the weaker Liggett hypotheses are useful beyond Kingman's original stationary-array assumptions. Pitfall: The stronger all-triples subadditivity can fail because the fastest later descendant need not descend from the fastest earlier individual.

front: Branching speed via lifetime large deviations (6.5 Applications)
back: For mean offspring mu and lifetime sums with rate c(a), the asymptotic earliest-generation speed is gamma=inf{a: log mu - c(a)>0}. Exam use: Connects subadditive limits with large-deviation rate functions. Pitfall: The subadditive theorem gives existence; this formula requires additional branching and large-deviation analysis.

front: Invariant sigma-field and invariant random variables (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Show invariant events form a sigma-field by pullback closure; prove an I-measurable random variable is invariant by checking rational cuts, and conversely use invariant inverse images. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.1; identifying E(X|I) and invariant limits. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Strict representative for almost invariant sets (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: From an almost invariant set, form a forward pullback union B and then C=intersection phi^{-n}B; C is strictly invariant and differs only by a null set. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.2; replacing mod-null invariance by exact invariance. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Direct density proof of irrational rotation ergodicity (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Use pigeonhole gaps to prove the irrational orbit is dense, approximate positive-measure sets by high-density intervals, and contradict coexistence of invariant A and A^c with positive measure. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.3; proving ergodicity without Fourier series. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Two-sided extension of a stationary sequence (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Define finite-dimensional laws on integer-indexed coordinates by shifting all indices into the nonnegative side, then use stationarity for consistency and Kolmogorov extension. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.4; return-time and cycle arguments needing negative times. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Stationarity and ergodicity pass to factors (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: For Y_k=g(X_k,X_{k+1},...), stationarity follows from shifted future laws; invariant events for Y pull back to invariant events for X, so ergodicity passes to Y. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.5; creating stationary derived processes. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Random phase makes iid blocks stationary and ergodic (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: Concatenate iid blocks and start at an independent uniform phase; one-step shifts rotate the phase and occasionally shift the iid block sequence, giving stationarity and ergodicity. Exam use: Exercise 6.1.6; block constructions with stationary output. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Gauss map invariant density by branch telescoping (6.1 Exercises: Stationarity, invariant events, ergodicity, and measure-preserving examples)
back: For phi(x)=1/x-floor(1/x), split into branches x=1/(k+y); the transformed density sum telescopes to 1/(1+y). Exam use: Exercise 6.1.7; verifying the continued-fraction invariant measure. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Lp upgrade of Birkhoff by truncation (6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities)
back: Prove Lp convergence for bounded observables by bounded convergence, then truncate X and control the tail using Jensen plus Lp contraction of conditional expectation. Exam use: Exercise 6.2.1; upgrading ergodic averages from L1 to Lp. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Moving observable ergodic average (6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities)
back: Decompose averages of g_m(phi^m) into the fixed g average plus errors; control a.s. errors by tail suprema and L1 errors by Cesaro means. Exam use: Exercise 6.2.2; triangular or time-varying observables in ergodic averages. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Wiener maximal inequality from maximal ergodic lemma (6.2 Exercises: Birkhoff upgrades, moving observables, and maximal inequalities)
back: Apply the maximal ergodic lemma to Y=X-alpha so the positivity event of the shifted partial sums is exactly {max S_j/j > alpha}. Exam use: Exercise 6.2.3; deriving maximal probability bounds for ergodic averages. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Range expectation by last-visit decomposition (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: Count each visited site by its last visit time; stationarity makes the probability of no future revisit over n-m steps equal to g_{n-m}. Exam use: Exercise 6.3.1; expected range identities for stationary-increment walks. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Positive drift escape probability from range growth (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: With ergodic integer increments bounded above by one and positive mean, the range grows at the same rate as the running maximum; Theorem 6.3.1 identifies the rate with P(escape). Exam use: Exercise 6.3.2; recurrence/escape probabilities under stationary increments. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Cycle trick occupation ratio (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: Use a two-sided stationary version and partition by the last visit to A before time 0; expected B-occupation in an A-cycle equals P(X0 in B)/P(X0 in A). Exam use: Exercise 6.3.3; constructing stationary measures from excursions. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Stationary renewal first-waiting bias (6.3 Exercises: Range growth, recurrence, cycle tricks, and stationary renewal bias)
back: Under conditioning on a renewal at time 0, stationarity of return gaps gives P(T=n)=Pbar(T>=n)/Ebar T for the waiting time seen from a stationary time. Exam use: Exercise 6.3.4; inspection-paradox and stationary renewal calculations. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Arbitrarily slow deterministic subadditive convergence (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Set X_{m,m+k}=f(k) with f(k)/k decreasing; subadditivity follows by comparing both pieces to f(n)/n, so the expectation convergence can be as slow as f(n)/n. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.1; understanding limits of Fekete-type convergence rates. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Longest common subsequence first-moment bounds (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Compute small-n expectations for lower bounds and bound P(L_n >= K) by the expected number of matching index-pair subsequences, binom(n,K)^2 2^{-K}. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.2; bounding LCS constants. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Poisson greedy path lower bound (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Choose successive Poisson points minimizing x+y in the northeast quadrant; the increment sum has tail exp(-t^2/2), giving mean coordinate step sqrt(pi/8) and gamma >= sqrt(8/pi). Exam use: Exercise 6.5.3; lower bounds for Hammersley's increasing path constant. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Permutation LIS upper bound by first moment (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: For a fixed k-subset of indices, the values are increasing with probability 1/k!, so E J_k^n=binom(n,k)/k!; Stirling with k~alpha sqrt(n) gives gamma <= e. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.4; upper bounds for longest increasing subsequences. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Branching Laplace martingale speed bound (6.5 Exercises: Applications of subadditivity to LCS, LIS, Poisson paths, and branching)
back: Normalize the generation-n Laplace sum by (mu phi(theta))^n to get a nonnegative martingale; Markov's inequality bounds early birth events when exp(-theta a)/(mu phi(theta))>1. Exam use: Exercise 6.5.5; age-dependent branching speed upper-tail bounds. Pitfall: Check stationarity, ergodicity, integrability, and indexing hypotheses before reusing the pattern.

front: Chapter 7: Brownian motion definition
back: A Brownian motion has independent increments, Gaussian increments B(s+t)-B(s) with mean 0 and variance t, and almost surely continuous sample paths. Exam use: Use as the base checklist whenever a process is claimed to be Brownian. Pitfall: Independent Gaussian increments alone do not automatically give continuous paths unless a continuous modification is specified.

front: Chapter 7: Translation invariance of increments
back: The process {B_t-B_0:t>=0} is independent of B_0 and has the law of Brownian motion started at 0. Exam use: Lets hitting and exit calculations reduce to a Brownian motion started at 0 or at the current location. Pitfall: This is spatial translation of the starting point, not time-shift Markov conditioning.

front: Chapter 7: Brownian scaling
back: For c>0 and B_0=0, {B_{cs}:s>=0} has the same finite-dimensional distributions as {sqrt(c) B_s:s>=0}. Exam use: High-yield tool for hitting-time laws, path regularity, and rescaling exit probabilities. Pitfall: Scaling changes time by c and space by sqrt(c); reversing these powers is a common error.

front: Chapter 7: Gaussian process characterization
back: A continuous Gaussian process with mean 0 and covariance E[B_s B_t]=s wedge t is Brownian motion started at 0. Exam use: Use to prove transformed processes are Brownian, especially time inversion. Pitfall: The covariance condition alone is not enough without the Gaussian finite-dimensional law and continuity.

front: Chapter 7: Kolmogorov extension construction
back: Brownian finite-dimensional distributions are built from Gaussian transition densities and then extended consistently to a path-space probability law. Exam use: Explains why the formal process exists before path properties are studied. Pitfall: The raw product path space can make continuity nonmeasurable; the continuous-path space fixes the measurable model.

front: Chapter 7: Kolmogorov continuity criterion
back: If E|X_t-X_s|^beta <= K|t-s|^{1+alpha}, then dyadic paths admit a Holder-continuous version of any exponent gamma<alpha/beta. Exam use: Use moment bounds to prove sample-path continuity for Gaussian and process constructions. Pitfall: The exponent comes from alpha/beta, not beta/alpha; endpoint exponents are usually not obtained.

front: Chapter 7: Brownian Holder continuity below one half
back: Brownian paths are almost surely Holder continuous on compact intervals for every exponent gamma<1/2. Exam use: Useful for justifying pathwise approximations while remembering Brownian paths are rough. Pitfall: The exponent 1/2 itself is not included.

front: Chapter 7: Brownian paths are nowhere differentiable
back: With probability one, Brownian paths are not Lipschitz continuous, and hence not differentiable, at any time. Exam use: Prevents calculus-style manipulation of B_t and motivates quadratic variation and Ito calculus.
